1	---
2	name: blueprint-clean
3	description: Post-write blueprint gate. Strips Lean leakage, project history, and verbosity. Validates and inserts missing source quotes from PDFs/TeXs.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: true
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch this subagent AFTER a blueprint-writer round and BEFORE dispatching provers.
10	  - Required to ensure blueprint purity (math-only).
11	  - Include `references/**` in its write-domain so it can spawn `reference-retriever` if needed.
12	---
13	
14	# Blueprint Clean Subagent
15	
16	Your job is to read and refine a blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) to ensure it meets strict quality, purity, and citation standards.
17	
18	## Your Tasks
19	
20	1. **Strip Lean Leakage & Project History:**
21	   - Remove any mention of Lean tactics, typeclass notes, or specific Lean implementation strategies.
22	   - Remove project history references like "since iteration N", "our failed route", etc.
23	   - Ensure the blueprint reads as a timeless, standalone mathematical document.
24	2. **Trim Verbosity:**
25	   - Remove useless, overly conversational paragraphs.
26	   - Remove passages that are irrelevant or redundantly discuss things already implemented, unless they serve a mathematical purpose.
27	   - Keep the prose concise and mathematically precise.
28	3. **Enforce Citation Discipline & Add Quotes:**
29	   - Verify every block derived from a reference has a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`.
30	   - **If the quote is missing** but the reference exists in `references/`, you MUST open the actual reference `.pdf` or `.tex` file (using the `.md` index file as a pointer to find the section/page), extract the verbatim quote, and add it to the blueprint.
31	   - **If a reference SHOULD be cited** but isn't mentioned by the writer, and the source is missing from `references/`, spawn a `reference-retriever` to fetch it.
32	4. **Fix LaTeX:**
33	   - Correct basic LaTeX syntax errors.
34	   - Ensure `\uses{}` and `\label{}` cross-references are formatted correctly.
35	
36	## Spawning a Retriever
37	If you identify a missing reference, spawn it using:
38	```bash
39	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py --name reference-retriever --slug <slug> --directive-file <file> --write-domain 'references/**'
40	```
41	
42	Return your outcome and the path to your report.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Tags 02KE
5	% (Quasi-coherence of higher direct images), 02KG (relative affine vanishing),
6	% and 02KH (Flat base change) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
7	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
8	
9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
37	
38	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
39	
40	\begin{definition}[Higher direct image]
41	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
44	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
45	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
46	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
47	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
48	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
49	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
50	  % the sheaf
51	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
52	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
53	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
54	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
55	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
56	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
57	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
58	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
59	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
60	  sheafification of the presheaf
61	  \[
62	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
63	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
64	  \]
65	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
66	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{Quasi-coherence of the higher direct images}
70	
71	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherence of higher direct images]
72	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}
73	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_isQuasiCoherent}
74	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
75	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KE (Lemma
76	  % `lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images', part (1))
77	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L741--757).
78	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
79	  % Assume that $f$ is quasi-separated and quasi-compact.
80	  % \begin{enumerate}
81	  % \item For any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ the
82	  % higher direct images $R^pf_*\mathcal{F}$ are quasi-coherent on $S$.
83	  % \item If $S$ is quasi-compact, there exists an integer $n = n(X, S, f)$
84	  % such that $R^pf_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $p \geq n$ and any
85	  % quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}$ on $X$.
86	  % \item In fact, if $S$ is quasi-compact we can find $n = n(X, S, f)$
87	  % such that for every
88	  % morphism of schemes $S' \to S$ we have $R^p(f')_*\mathcal{F}' = 0$
89	  % for $p \geq n$ and any quasi-coherent sheaf $\mathcal{F}'$
90	  % on $X'$. Here $f' : X' = S' \times_S X \to S'$ is the base change of $f$.
91	  % \end{enumerate}"
92	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KE (``Quasi-coherence of higher direct
93	  images''), part (1).}
94	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated morphism of schemes.
95	  Then for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) and every
96	  \(p \geq 0\), the higher direct image \(R^p f_*\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent
97	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module.
98	\end{lemma}
99	
100	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We first prove (1). Note that under the hypotheses of the
101	% lemma the sheaf
102	% $R^0f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent by
103	% Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}.
104	% Using
105	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-localize-higher-direct-images}
106	% we see that forming higher direct images commutes with restriction
107	% to open subschemes. Since being quasi-coherent is local on $S$ we
108	% reduce to the case discussed in the next paragraph.
109	%
110	% Proof of (1) in case $S$ is affine. We will use the induction principle.
111	% Since $f$ quasi-compact and quasi-separated we see that $X$
112	% is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. For $U \subset X$
113	% quasi-compact open and $a = f|_U$ we let $P(U)$ be the property that
114	% $R^pa_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent on $S$ for all quasi-coherent modules
115	% $\mathcal{F}$ on $U$ and all $p \geq 0$. Since $P(X)$ is (1), it suffices
116	% the prove conditions (1) and (2) of Lemma \ref{lemma-induction-principle} hold.
117	% If $U$ is affine, then $P(U)$ holds because $R^pa_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for
118	% $p \geq 1$ (by  Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} and
119	% Morphisms, Lemma \ref{morphisms-lemma-morphism-affines-affine})
120	% and we've already observed the result holds for $p = 0$ in the first
121	% paragraph. Next, let $U \subset X$ be a quasi-compact open, $V \subset X$
122	% an affine open, and assume $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ hold.
123	% Let $a = f|_U$, $b = f|_V$, $c = f|_{U \cap V}$, and $g = f|_{U \cup V}$.
124	% Then for any quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_{U \cup V}$-module $\mathcal{F}$
125	% we have the relative Mayer-Vietoris sequence
126	% $$
127	% 0 \to
128	% g_*\mathcal{F} \to
129	% a_*(\mathcal{F}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{F}|_V) \to
130	% c_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V}) \to
131	% R^1g_*\mathcal{F} \to \ldots
132	% $$
133	% see Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-relative-mayer-vietoris}.
134	% By $P(U)$, $P(V)$, $P(U \cap V)$ we see that
135	% $R^pa_*(\mathcal{F}|_U)$, $R^pb_*(\mathcal{F}|_V)$ and
136	% $R^pc_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U \cap V})$ are all quasi-coherent.
137	% Using the results on quasi-coherent sheaves in
138	% Schemes, Section \ref{schemes-section-quasi-coherent}
139	% this implies that each of the sheaves
140	% $R^pg_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent since it sits in the middle of a short
141	% exact sequence with a cokernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves
142	% on the left and a kernel of a map between quasi-coherent sheaves on the right.
143	% Whence $P(U \cup V)$ and the proof of (1) is complete."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
146	  Quasi-coherence is local on \(S\), and forming higher direct images commutes
147	  with restriction to open subschemes, so we may assume \(S\) is affine; then
148	  \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. We argue by the induction principle
149	  for quasi-compact opens of a quasi-compact quasi-separated scheme: for a
150	  quasi-compact open \(U \subset X\) with \(a = f|_U\), let \(P(U)\) be the
151	  property that \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent on \(S\) for every
152	  quasi-coherent module \(\mathcal{G}\) on \(U\) and every \(p \geq 0\); the
153	  desired statement is \(P(X)\).
154	
155	  \emph{Affine base.} If \(U\) is affine then \(a\) is an affine morphism, so
156	  \(R^p a_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) for \(p \geq 1\) by the relative affine vanishing of
157	  Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}, while
158	  \(R^0 a_*\mathcal{G} = a_*\mathcal{G}\) is quasi-coherent because the pushforward
159	  of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated morphism is
160	  quasi-coherent. Hence \(P(U)\) holds.
161	
162	  \emph{Inductive step.} Let \(U \subset X\) be quasi-compact open, \(V \subset X\)
163	  affine open, and suppose \(P(U)\), \(P(V)\), \(P(U \cap V)\) hold. Writing
164	  \(a = f|_U\), \(b = f|_V\), \(c = f|_{U \cap V}\), \(h = f|_{U \cup V}\), the
165	  relative Mayer--Vietoris sequence
166	  \[
167	    0 \to h_*\mathcal{G} \to a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U) \oplus b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)
168	      \to c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V}) \to R^1 h_*\mathcal{G} \to \cdots
169	  \]
170	  exhibits each \(R^p h_*\mathcal{G}\) as an extension built from quasi-coherent
171	  sheaves \(R^p a_*(\mathcal{G}|_U)\), \(R^p b_*(\mathcal{G}|_V)\),
172	  \(R^p c_*(\mathcal{G}|_{U \cap V})\): it sits between the cokernel of a map of
173	  quasi-coherent sheaves and the kernel of a map of quasi-coherent sheaves, hence
174	  is quasi-coherent. Thus \(P(U \cup V)\) holds. By the induction principle
175	  \(P(X)\) holds, proving the lemma.
176	\end{proof}

178	\section{Vanishing of higher direct images along an affine morphism}
179	
180	\begin{lemma}[Relative affine vanishing]
181	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_affine_vanishing}
182	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_affine_eq_zero}
183	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
184	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KG (Lemma
185	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing')
186	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L180--185).
187	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
188	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
189	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
190	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KG (``Relative affine vanishing'').}
191	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) a
192	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. If \(f\) is affine, then
193	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\).
194	\end{lemma}
195	
196	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
197	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
198	% the sheaf
199	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
200	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
201	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
202	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
203	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
204	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
205	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
206	% opens we win."
207	\begin{proof}
208	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image}
209	  By Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}, \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the
210	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),
211	  \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})\). Since \(f\) is affine, the preimage \(f^{-1}(V)\) of
212	  any affine open \(V \subset S\) is affine. The higher cohomology of a
213	  quasi-coherent sheaf on an affine scheme vanishes, so
214	  \(H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for all \(i \geq 1\) and all
215	  affine \(V\). As the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), the sheafification of a
216	  presheaf that vanishes on a basis is zero; hence \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F} = 0\) for
217	  all \(i \geq 1\).
218	\end{proof}
219	
220	\section{Flat base change for the higher direct images}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}[Flat base change, $i \geq 1$ case]
223	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_higher}
224	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_higherDirectImage_isIso}
225	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
226	    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
227	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change"), the
228	  % $i \geq 1$ case (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
229	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
230	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
231	  % $$
232	  % \xymatrix{
233	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
234	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
235	  % }
236	  % $$
237	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
238	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
239	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
240	  % For any $i \geq 0$
241	  % \begin{enumerate}
242	  % \item the base change map of
243	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
244	  % is an isomorphism
245	  % $$
246	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
247	  % $$
248	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
249	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
250	  % \end{enumerate}"
251	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
252	  \(i \geq 1\) case.}
253	  Consider the cartesian square
254	  \[
255	    \begin{array}{ccc}
256	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
257	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
258	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
259	    \end{array}
260	  \]
261	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
262	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\) is
263	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then for every \(i \geq 1\) the canonical
264	  base-change map (the higher-degree analogue of the pushforward base-change map
265	  of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is an isomorphism
266	  \[
267	    g^*\bigl(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
268	    R^i f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
269	  \]
270	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
271	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map of
272	  \(B\)-modules
273	  \[
274	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
275	    H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
276	  \]
277	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
278	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
279	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
280	\end{theorem}
281	
282	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
283	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
284	% and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
285	% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
286	% Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
287	% (Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
288	% it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
289	% $A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
290	% (equality by
291	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
292	% Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
293	% $\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
294	% $H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
295	% to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
296	% (Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
297	% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
298	%
299	% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
300	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
301	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
302	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
303	% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
304	% We are trying to show that the map
305	% $$
306	% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
307	% $$
308	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
309	% is an isomorphism.
310	%
311	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
312	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
313	% $$
314	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
315	% $$
316	% see
317	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
318	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
319	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
320	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
321	% $$
322	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
323	% $$"
324	\begin{proof}
325	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent,
326	    def:pushforward_base_change_map}
327	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The assertion is local on \(S'\), so we
328	  may assume \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with
329	  \(A \to B\) flat. By quasi-coherence of the higher direct images
330	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_quasi_coherent}), \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is
331	  the quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module associated to the \(A\)-module
332	  \(H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})\), and likewise
333	  \(R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is associated to the \(B\)-module
334	  \(H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
335	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map is identified with the
336	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
337	  \[
338	    H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
339	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
340	  \]
341	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
342	
343	  \medskip
344	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
345	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
346	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness every finite
347	  intersection \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) is again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
348	  computes sheaf cohomology:
349	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\) for all \(p\).
350	  Base changing the cover yields an affine open cover
351	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
352	  \(X_B\), with the same property
353	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
354	  affine case identifies each term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the
355	  original term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of
356	  cochain complexes
357	  \[
358	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
359	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
360	  \]
361	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, the functor \(- \otimes_A B\) is exact and hence
362	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^i\) in any
363	  degree \(i \geq 1\) gives the desired isomorphism
364	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
365	
366	  \medskip
367	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
368	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
369	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated rather
370	  than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so flat base
371	  change holds for each of them. Comparing the two {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
372	  sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
373	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
374	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
375	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively, the
376	  separated case provides the term-by-term identification
377	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
378	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
379	  \(E_2\)-pages; flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through the
380	  spectral sequences to the abutment, giving
381	  \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) in every degree
382	  \(i \geq 1\). Translating back to sheaves on \(S'\) yields the asserted
383	  isomorphism \(g^*(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\sim} R^i f'_*\mathcal{F}'\).
384	\end{proof}

1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean
4	
5	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
6	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
7	
8	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
9	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
10	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
11	\[
12	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
13	  \qquad
14	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
15	\]
16	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
17	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
18	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
19	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
20	
21	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
22	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
23	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
24	% groups)
25	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
26	% SOURCE QUOTE:
27	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
28	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
29	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
30	% abelian groups defined by the formula
31	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
32	% \end{dfn}
33	%  [\dots]
34	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
35	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
36	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
37	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
38	% topologies  by
39	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
40	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
41	%  \end{dfn}"
42	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
43	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
44	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
45	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
46	homomorphisms.}
47	
48	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
49	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
50	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
51	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
52	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
53	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
54	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
55	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
56	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
57	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
58	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
59	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
60	structure. This is the content of
61	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
62	
63	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
64	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
65	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
66	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
67	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
68	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
69	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
70	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
71	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
72	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
73	\begin{enumerate}
74	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
75	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
76	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
77	    isomorphism classes;
78	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
79	    for \(\otimes\);
80	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
81	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
82	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
83	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
84	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
85	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
86	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
87	\end{enumerate}
88	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
89	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
90	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
91	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
92	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
93	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
94	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
95	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
96	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
97	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
98	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
99	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
100	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
101	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
102	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
103	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
104	supplied.
105	
106	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
107	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
108	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
109	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
110	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
111	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
112	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
113	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
114	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
115	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
116	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
117	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
118	assembles \emph{by hand} the commutative group of \(\otimes\)-invertible
119	\(\otimes\)-iso-classes --- the Picard group
120	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) --- each group-axiom field fed a single
121	existence-of-isomorphism. This is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
122	fixed-ring \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\),
123	whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent
124	monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) that
125	Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring; the present construction cannot take that
126	\(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route, since the coherent monoidal category on
127	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is exactly what it
128	avoids. The
129	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
130	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
131	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
132	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
133	
134	\section{Mathlib API survey}
135	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
136	
137	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
138	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
139	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
140	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
141	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
142	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
143	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
144	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
145	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
146	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
147	source.
148	
149	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
150	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
151	monoidal-category instance
152	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
153	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
154	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
155	\[
156	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
157	\]
158	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct` /
159	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
160	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
161	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
162	% SOURCE QUOTE:
163	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
164	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
165	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
166	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
167	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
168	%   ...
169	%   tensorUnit := unit _
170	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
171	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
172	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
173	\textit{Source: [Mathlib], \texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct} /
174	\texttt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}, in
175	\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean}, L104--L125.}
176	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
177	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
178	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
179	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
180	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
181	\(\mathcal{O}_X\) --- no project work. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
182	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
183	
184	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
185	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
186	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
187	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
188	the whiskering-stability data:
189	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
190	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
191	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
192	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
193	% SOURCE QUOTE:
194	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
195	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
196	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)
197	%  ...
198	% def LocalizedMonoidal (L : C ⥤ D) (W : MorphismProperty C)
199	%     [W.IsMonoidal] [L.IsLocalization W] {unit : D} (_ : L.obj (𝟙_ C) ≅ unit) := D
200	%  ...
201	% noncomputable instance :
202	%     MonoidalCategory (LocalizedMonoidal L W ε) where ..."

204	\texttt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}, in

206	Given \([\MonoidalCategory\,C]\), \([W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}]\), a localization
207	functor \(L : C \to D\) with \([L.\mathtt{IsLocalization}\,W]\) and an isomorphism
208	\(\varepsilon : L(\mathbf{1}_C) \cong \mathtt{unit}\), the localized category
209	\(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\,L\,W\,\varepsilon\) is a \(\MonoidalCategory\) with
210	\emph{all} coherence (associator, unitors, pentagon, triangle, naturalities)
211	derived. There is \emph{no} \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) anywhere in the
212	hypothesis chain.
213	
214	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
215	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
216	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
217	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
218	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
219	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:
220	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W`
221	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
222	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
223	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
224	% SOURCE QUOTE:
225	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
226	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
227	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
228	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
229	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢
230	%     intro Φ
231	%     rw [Functor.Monoidal.map_tensor]
232	%     infer_instance)"
233	\textit{Source: [Mathlib],
234	\texttt{ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal\_W}, in
235	\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean}, L48--L57.}
236	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
237	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
238	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
239	\(A\).
240	
241	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
242	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
243	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
244	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
245	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
246	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
247	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
248	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
249	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
250	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
251	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
252	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
253	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
254	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
255	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
256	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
257	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
258	critical path.
259	
260	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
261	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
262	is already present in Mathlib: the file
263	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
264	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
265	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
266	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
267	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
268	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
269	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
270	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
271	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
272	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
273	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
274	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
275	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
276	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
277	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
278	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
279	
280	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
281	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
282	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
283	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
284	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
285	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
286	independently of any upstream timing.
287	
288	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
289	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
290	
291	\begin{definition}
292	
293	\leanok
294	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
295	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
297	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
298	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
299	  \[
300	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
301	  \]
302	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
303	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
304	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
305	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
306	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
307	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
308	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
309	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
310	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
311	  canonical compatibility
312	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
313	     \;\cong\;
314	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
315	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
316	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
317	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
318	
319	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
320	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
321	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
322	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
323	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
324	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
325	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
326	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
327	\end{definition}
328	
329	\begin{lemma}
330	
331	\leanok
332	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
333	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
334	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
335	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
336	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
337	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
338	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
339	  \[
340	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
341	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
342	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
343	  \]
344	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
345	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
346	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
347	  identities and composition. This bifunctorial action on morphisms is
348	  the only datum the present lemma provides. The further coherence data
349	  of a monoidal structure --- the unitors
350	  \[
351	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
352	    \qquad
353	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
354	  \]
355	  the associator
356	  \(\alpha_{M, N, P} : (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \xrightarrow{\sim}
357	   M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\), and the braiding
358	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\) ---
359	  are \emph{not} outputs of this lemma; they are off the critical path,
360	  recorded in \cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}.
361	\end{lemma}
362	
363	\begin{proof}
364	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
365	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
366	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
367	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial. The bifunctorial action
368	  on morphisms glues across the cover because the underlying gluing data of
369	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect the canonical morphisms on
370	  overlaps; equivalently, the morphism action is inherited from
371	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
372	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
373	  sheafification. The unitor/associator/braiding coherence data are
374	  \emph{not} constructed here; they are off the critical path
375	  (\cref{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}).
376	\end{proof}
377	
378	\begin{remark}
379	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
380	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
381	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
382	        lem:whisker_of_W}
383	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
384	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
385	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
386	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
387	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
388	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
389	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
390	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
391	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
392	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
393	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
394	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
395	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
396	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
397	
398	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
399	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
400	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
401	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
402	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
403	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
404	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
405	  fixed-base presheaves in
406	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
407	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
408	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
409	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
410	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
411	  it.
412	
413	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
414	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
415	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
416	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
417	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
418	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
419	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
420	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
421	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
422	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
423	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
424	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
425	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
426	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). The carrier --- iso-classes
427	  of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\) --- is the scheme-theoretic analogue of
428	  Mathlib's Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
429	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}), but it
430	  is built \emph{differently}: Mathlib reads its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) off the
431	  \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the coherent monoidal category
432	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) over a fixed ring,
433	  whereas here --- with no coherent monoidal category on
434	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) available for the varying structure sheaf --- the
435	  group is assembled by hand from the existence-of-isomorphism facts.
436	\end{remark}
437	
438	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
439	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
440	
441	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
442	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
443	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
444	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
445	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
446	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
447	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
448	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
449	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
450	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
451	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
452	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
453	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
454	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
455	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
456	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
457	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
458	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
459	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
460	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
461	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
462	to the off-path route~(e).
463	
464	\begin{lemma}
465	
466	\leanok
467	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
468	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
469	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
470	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
471	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
472	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
473	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
474	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
475	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
476	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
477	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
478	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
479	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
480	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
481	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
482	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
483	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
484	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
485	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
486	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
487	  \[
488	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
489	      \;\;:=\;\;
490	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
491	  \]
492	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
493	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
494	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
495	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
496	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
497	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
498	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
499	\end{lemma}
500	
501	\begin{proof}
502	
503	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
504	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
505	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
506	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
507	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
508	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
509	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
510	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
511	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
512	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
513	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
514	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
515	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
516	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
517	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
518	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
519	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
520	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
521	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
522	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq
523	  transport across the definitional unfolding of \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\).
524	
525	  The commutativity hypothesis is essential: the presheaf-of-modules monoidal
526	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the
527	  instance must be stated over \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-factored \(R, S\). In
528	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
529	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
530	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.
531	\end{proof}
