533	\begin{lemma}
534	
535	\leanok
536	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
537	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
538	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
539	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
540	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
541	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
542	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
543	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
544	  morphism
545	  \[
546	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
547	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
548	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
549	  \]
550	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
551	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
552	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
553	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
554	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
555	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
556	\end{lemma}
557	
558	\begin{proof}
559	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
560	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
561	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
562	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
563	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
564	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
565	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
566	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
567	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
568	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
569	  isomorphism}
570	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
571	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
572	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
573	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
574	  therefore extension of scalars along
575	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
576	
577	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
578	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
579	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
580	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
581	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
582	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
583	  the abstract pullback functor.
584	
585	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
586	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
587	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
588	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
589	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
590	   \;\cong\;
591	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
592	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
593	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
594	  comparison
595	  \[
596	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
597	      \;\longrightarrow\;
598	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
599	      \;\otimes\;
600	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
601	  \]
602	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
603	
604	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
605	  % \ref{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback} (the pair $(f^*, f_*)$ of
606	  % pullback/pushforward of $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism of ringed
607	  % spaces is an adjoint pair)
608	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L252-273).
609	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
610	  % "\begin{lemma}
611	  % \label{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}
612	  % Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$
613	  % be a morphism of ringed spaces.
614	  % \begin{enumerate}
615	  % \item The functor
616	  % $f_* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y)$
617	  % is left exact. In fact it commutes with all limits.
618	  % \item The functor
619	  % $f^* : \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_Y) \to \textit{Mod}(\mathcal{O}_X)$
620	  % is right exact. In fact it commutes with all colimits.
621	  %  [\dots]
622	  % \end{enumerate}
623	  % \end{lemma}
624	  %  [\dots]
625	  % Parts (1) and (2) hold because $(f^*, f_*)$ is an adjoint pair
626	  % of functors, see
627	  % Sheaves, Lemma \ref{sheaves-lemma-adjoint-pullback-pushforward-modules}"
628	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Modules on Ringed Spaces, Lemma
629	  \texttt{lemma-exactness-pushforward-pullback}: pullback and pushforward of
630	  $\mathcal{O}$-modules along a morphism form an adjoint pair $(f^*, f_*)$, the
631	  abstract universal property on which the comparison below rests.}
632	
633	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
634	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
635	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
636	  adjoint of pushforward (the presheaf-of-modules avatar of the
637	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) adjunction of the cited Stacks lemma) and admits no
638	  sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned commit. The closure passes through
639	  two presheaf-level ingredients, of which exactly one now remains open.
640	
641	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
642	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
643	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
644	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
645	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
646	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
647	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
648	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
649	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
650	  \otimes_S N)\).
651	
652	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
653	  \[
654	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
655	  \]
656	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\). One exhibits a
657	  presheaf-level adjunction
658	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
659	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
660	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
661	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
662	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
663	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
664	  composing the resulting comparison with
665	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
666	
667	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
668	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
669	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
670	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
671	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
672	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
673	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
674	  presheaf level. This residual is being constructed \emph{directly}, mirroring the
675	  existing sheaf-level template app-for-app at the presheaf level (a Mathlib-gradient
676	  build, not a deferral to an upstream PR).
677	
678	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law for
679	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules \(M, N\): the associator
680	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} consumes the comparison
681	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_f \cong M|_f \otimes_U N|_f\) on its sole consumer
682	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}, where the restriction is commuted
683	  past the \emph{sheafified} tensor \(M \otimes_X N\) \emph{before} any local
684	  trivialisation of \(M, N\) is introduced --- so the comparison is required for the
685	  global, untrivialised modules, and no free-cover specialisation can stand in for it.
686	  Consequently the H1 residual \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong
687	  \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) is genuinely on the critical path, and closing it is
688	  the \emph{single open substrate obligation} of this chapter. The superseded
689	  route~(e) \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal} \to \mathtt{Sheaf.monoidalCategory}\)
690	  (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}) remains named only as a last-resort fallback should the
691	  presheaf-level adjunction itself bottom out; it is not the plan.
692	\end{proof}

694	\begin{lemma}
695	[Presheaf-level pushforward adjunction and strong-monoidal restriction (H1/H2 substrate)]
696	  \label{lem:presheaf_pushforward_adj_substrate}
697	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans,
698	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr,
699	        PresheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj,
700	        PresheafOfModules.isIso_of_isIso_app,
701	        PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}
702	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
703	  These five presheaf-level declarations are the two ingredients on which the
704	  linchpin \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} rests; both are absent from Mathlib at
705	  the pinned commit and are supplied project-side.
706	
707	  \emph{H1 (the pushforward adjunction).} For a morphism \(\beta\) of presheaves of
708	  rings, \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\) are the
709	  presheaf-level de-sheafifications of Mathlib's sheaf-level
710	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
711	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardCongr}\)
712	  (\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}),
713	  reproduced app-for-app one level down at the presheaf of modules. From them
714	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\) assembles the presheaf-level adjunction
715	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), the
716	  presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
717	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\). Pairing this against the
718	  existing \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackPushforwardAdjunction}\) through
719	  \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) yields the canonical isomorphism
720	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\) of two left
721	  adjoints of the same pushforward --- exactly the residual identified in Step~3 of
722	  the proof of \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
723	
724	  \emph{H2 (strong-monoidal restriction).} \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_isIso\_app}\) is the
725	  sectionwise isomorphism criterion: a morphism of presheaves of modules is an
726	  isomorphism once each of its section maps is. Built on it,
727	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfBijective}\) equips the presheaf-level
728	  restriction-of-scalars functor
729	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha\) with a \emph{strong}-monoidal
730	  structure whenever the base-change comparison \(\alpha\) is \emph{sectionwise
731	  bijective} --- the presheaf-level lift of the ModuleCat-level H2 core
732	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}, assembled section-by-section via
733	  \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\). It is to be distinguished from the
734	  module-level, ring-equivalence form
735	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\) already pinned in
736	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}: the present declaration is the
737	  \emph{presheaf}-level, \emph{bijective}-comparison form, the one the substrate lift
738	  actually consumes (where the comparison map
739	  \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)
740	  morphism but is not syntactically a \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\)).
741	
742	  Together H1 and H2 are the two Mathlib-absent ingredients the linchpin needed; they
743	  are self-contained presheaf-level results and are natural upstream-PR candidates.
744	\end{lemma}

746	\begin{lemma}
747	
748	\leanok
749	[Base change along a ring isomorphism commutes with $\otimes$]
750	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
751	  \lean{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}
752	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M, N\) be
753	  \(S\)-modules. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the tensor
754	  product: the canonical map
755	  \[
756	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N)
757	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
758	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \otimes_S N\bigr),
759	    \qquad a \otimes_R b \;\longmapsto\; a \otimes_S b,
760	  \]
761	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence. Equivalently, \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\) is
762	  \emph{strong} monoidal, not merely lax, when \(e\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}:
763	  base change along an isomorphism \(R \simeq S\) identifies the two ground rings, so
764	  the lax structure map of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) is invertible. The
765	  forward map is \(R\)-linear, but its inverse \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\)
766	  is only \emph{additive} (it is not \(S\)-linear), so the inverse is constructed as
767	  a homomorphism of additive groups and then verified to be the two-sided inverse of
768	  the forward \(R\)-linear map. This closes the strong-monoidal ingredient of
769	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, leaving the presheaf-level pushforward
770	  adjunction as the sole open residual there.
771	\end{lemma}
772	
773	\begin{lemma}
774	
775	[Strong-monoidal upgrade of \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism (ModuleCat-level H2 core)]
776	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_strongmonoidal}
777	  \lean{restrictScalars_isIso_μ, restrictScalars_isIso_ε,
778	        restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv,
779	        restrictScalars_isIso_μ_of_bijective,
780	        restrictScalars_isIso_ε_of_bijective}
781	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
782	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings and let
783	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom} :
784	  \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,S \to \mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R\) be the associated
785	  restriction-of-scalars functor, equipped with its canonical \emph{lax}-monoidal
786	  structure (the lax tensorator \(\mu\) and lax unit \(\varepsilon\) supplied by
787	  Mathlib). Then:
788	  \begin{enumerate}
789	    \item the lax tensorator
790	      \(\mu_{M, N} : \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \otimes_R \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e N
791	      \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M \otimes_S N)\) is an isomorphism
792	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu}\));
793	    \item the lax unit
794	      \(\varepsilon : \mathbf{1}_R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(\mathbf{1}_S)\)
795	      is an isomorphism (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon}\));
796	    \item consequently \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) is
797	      \emph{strong} monoidal: it carries the packaged structure
798	      \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
799	      (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsMonoidalOfRingEquiv}\)).
800	  \end{enumerate}
801	  The same two invertibility statements hold, more flexibly, under the hypothesis that
802	  the underlying ring homomorphism is merely \emph{bijective} rather than literally of
803	  the form \(e.\mathtt{toRingHom}\) for a packaged ring equivalence \(e\)
804	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\mu\_of\_bijective}\),
805	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isIso\_\varepsilon\_of\_bijective}\)); these bijective
806	  forms are the ones consumed by the presheaf-level lift, where the relevant
807	  comparison map \((\alpha.\mathtt{app}\,X).\mathtt{hom}\) is a bijective
808	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) morphism but is not syntactically a
809	  \((\,\cdot\,)\!.\mathtt{toRingHom}\).
810	\end{lemma}
811	
812	\begin{proof}
813	  \uses{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
814	  Statements (1) and (2) are the categorical repackaging of
815	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: when the ground-ring map is a ring
816	  \emph{isomorphism} the two ground rings are identified, so the lax structure maps
817	  \(\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\) of \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\) --- which are
818	  in general only one-directional --- become invertible. For (1) the inverse of
819	  \(\mu_{M, N}\) is the additive map \(a \otimes_S b \mapsto a \otimes_R b\) of
820	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}, verified to be two-sided inverse to
821	  the canonical \(R\)-linear \(\mu_{M, N}\); for (2) the unit comparison
822	  \(\mathbf{1}_R = R \to \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e S\) is the additive identification
823	  induced by \(e\). Strong monoidality (3) is then the Mathlib upgrade of a
824	  lax-monoidal functor all of whose structure maps are isomorphisms
825	  (\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\)), packaging \(\mu^{-1}, \varepsilon^{-1}\)
826	  into \((\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,e.\mathtt{toRingHom}).\mathtt{Monoidal}\).
827	  The bijective-hypothesis forms are proved identically, replacing ``\(e\) is a ring
828	  equivalence'' by ``the underlying ring homomorphism is bijective'' and reconstructing
829	  the inverse ground-ring map from bijectivity; this is what lets the presheaf-level
830	  lift feed in a section-wise-bijective comparison map directly.
831	
832	  These declarations constitute the \emph{ModuleCat-level H2}: strong
833	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along a ring isomorphism commutes with \(\otimes\). The
834	  one \emph{remaining} H2 step is the bounded, no-Mathlib-gap \emph{presheaf} lift:
835	  assembling \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\alpha).\mathtt{Monoidal}\)
836	  section-by-section via \(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.ofLaxMonoidal}\), using a
837	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-iso\,\(\Rightarrow\)\,bijective bridge to invoke the
838	  bijective forms above at each section, together with the
839	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) iso-iff-app-iso reflection to lift section-wise
840	  invertibility to the presheaf level; once that lift is in place,
841	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\) is monoidal. This presheaf H2 lift is bounded and
842	  has no Mathlib-absent ingredient; the sole genuinely Mathlib-absent piece of
843	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is the presheaf-level pushforward adjunction
844	  (``H1'').
845	\end{proof}

847	\begin{lemma}
848	
849	\leanok
850	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
851	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
852	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_isLocallyTrivial}
853	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
854	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
855	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
856	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
857	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
858	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
859	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
860	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
861	\end{lemma}
862	
863	\begin{proof}
864	
865	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
866	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
867	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
868	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
869	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
870	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
871	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
872	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
873	\end{proof}

888	[Flat whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
889	  \label{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
890	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
891	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
892	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the class of morphisms of presheaves of
893	  \(R\)-modules that are inverted by sheafification --- equivalently, the
894	  \emph{locally bijective} morphisms, i.e.\ those that are both locally injective
895	  and locally surjective for \(J\) (the characterisation
896	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). Let \(F\) be a presheaf of \(R\)-modules
897	  that is \emph{flat} (sectionwise: \(F(U)\) is a flat \(R(U)\)-module for every
898	  object \(U\) of the site). Then for every morphism \(g\) in \(J.W\) the
899	  left-whiskered morphism
900	  \[
901	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
902	  \]
903	  (the action of \(F \otimes^{p} -\) on \(g\), with \(\otimes^{p}\) the
904	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product) again lies in \(J.W\). By the symmetry of
905	  the presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, the right-whiskered morphism
906	  \(g \triangleright F\) likewise lies in \(J.W\) when \(F\) is flat.
907	
908	  Both halves --- the left-whiskering statement and its braiding-conjugate
909	  right-whiskering statement --- are established. They are valid standalone
910	  results about flat whiskering of sheafification-localizer morphisms. They are,
911	  however, \emph{off the critical path}: the live whiskering-stability data for
912	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (route~(e)) are the flatness-free variants
913	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_W}\)
914	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}), whose stalkwise proof holds for an \emph{arbitrary}
915	  whiskering object \(F\) and never invokes the sectionwise-flatness hypothesis
916	  of the present lemma. The sectionwise flatness condition --- \(F(U)\) flat over
917	  \(R(U)\) for \emph{every} object \(U\) of the site, including non-affine opens
918	  --- is not supplied by \(\otimes\)-invertibility or local triviality, which are
919	  affine-local properties; the present (flat) lemma is therefore retained as an
920	  independent, closed result, superseded on the critical path by the
921	  \(\mathtt{\_of\_W}\) variants of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
922	\end{lemma}
923	
924	\begin{proof}
925	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
926	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer
927	  (\(J.W\) is exactly the class of morphisms that are \(J\)-locally injective and
928	  \(J\)-locally surjective), it suffices to show that \(F \triangleleft g\) is
929	  both locally injective and locally surjective whenever \(g\) is.
930	
931	  The presheaf-of-modules tensor product is computed sectionwise,
932	  \((F \otimes^{p} G)(U) = F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} G(U)\), so for
933	  \(g : A \to B\) the whiskered morphism acts on sections over \(U\) as the
934	  \(R(U)\)-linear map
935	  \[
936	    \mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U) \;\colon\;
937	      F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} A(U) \;\longrightarrow\; F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U).
938	  \]
939	
940	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is automatic and needs no hypothesis on \(F\):
941	  tensoring is right exact, so applying \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the cokernel
942	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\); since \(g\) is locally
943	  surjective its cokernel is locally zero, hence so is the cokernel of
944	  \(F \triangleleft g\), and \(F \triangleleft g\) is locally surjective.
945	
946	  \emph{Local injectivity} is where flatness enters. Because each \(F(U)\) is a
947	  flat \(R(U)\)-module, the functor \(F(U) \otimes_{R(U)} -\) preserves injective
948	  linear maps (Mathlib
949	  \(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)). Thus
950	  whenever \(g(U)\) is injective on a covering sieve, so is
951	  \((\mathrm{id}_{F(U)} \otimes g(U))\); the local injectivity of \(g\) is
952	  therefore transported sectionwise to \(F \triangleleft g\).
953	
954	  Locally injective and locally surjective together give
955	  \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
956	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
957	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure, which is an
958	  isomorphism and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\); the flatness of \(F\) is
959	  the only input, and it is symmetric in the two tensor factors.
960	\end{proof}

1041	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}): apply

1043	presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) and the
1044	sheafification localizer \(J.W\). The single new input the API requires is the

1049	strategy, of which this route~(e) is the second tier. The \textbf{primary} tier

1081	[The $R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x$-linear stalk map of a morphism of presheaves of modules]
1082	  \label{lem:stalk_linear_map}
1083	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1084	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, \(R\) a presheaf of commutative rings on
1085	  \(X\), and \(x \in X\) a point. For a morphism \(g : M \to N\) of presheaves of
1086	  \(R\)-modules, the induced map on stalks
1087	  \[
1088	    g_x \;\colon\; M_x \;\longrightarrow\; N_x
1089	  \]
1090	  is \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\)), and is
1091	  characterised on germs by
1092	  \(g_x(\mathrm{germ}_x\,s) = \mathrm{germ}_x(g_U\,s)\)
1093	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_germ}\)). When the underlying map of
1094	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-valued stalks is an isomorphism, \(g_x\) is bijective
1095	  (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\)); in that case it bundles to
1096	  an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear equivalence
1097	  \(M_x \xrightarrow{\sim} N_x\) (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearEquivOfIsIso}\)).
1098	
1099	  These four declarations are the \emph{(d.1)-partial implementation} --- the
1100	  linearity half of ingredient (d.1) of
1101	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- consumed by the
1102	  \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step in the proof of that lemma. They build on the
1103	  stalk module structure of \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk};
1104	  the linearity packaging itself is project-original.
1105	\end{lemma}
1106	
1107	\begin{proof}
1108	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1109	  The stalk \(M_x = \mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) carries its
1110	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure from
1111	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk}. The underlying additive map
1112	  \(g_x\) on stalks is the filtered colimit of the section maps \(g_U\); since
1113	  each section map is \(R(U)\)-linear and the scalar action on the stalk is the
1114	  colimit of the section actions, \(g_x\) respects the
1115	  \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-action, which gives the linear map together with its
1116	  germ characterisation. Bijectivity is read off the underlying
1117	  \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk isomorphism (a bijective linear map is a linear
1118	  equivalence), and bundling the inverse yields the linear equivalence.
1119	\end{proof}

1137	[Local injectivity of a whiskered localizer morphism (stalkwise, flatness-free)]
1138	  \label{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1139	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1140	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a site carrying a Grothendieck
1141	  topology \(J\), and let \(J.W\) be the sheafification localizer (the locally
1142	  bijective morphisms). Concretely the site carries the
1143	  \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) property for \(\mathtt{Ab}\), so \(J.W\) is
1144	  exactly the class of \(J\)-locally bijective morphisms; this is recorded as a
1145	  typeclass hypothesis \([J.\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\,\mathtt{Ab}]\) on
1146	  the statement. Let \(F\) be an \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of
1147	  \(R\)-modules, and let \(g : M \to N\) be a morphism of presheaves of
1148	  \(R\)-modules whose underlying morphism of presheaves of abelian groups
1149	  \((\mathtt{toPresheaf}\,R).\mathrm{map}\,g\) lies in \(J.W\). Then the
1150	  left-whiskered morphism
1151	  \[
1152	    F \triangleleft g \;\;=\;\; \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g
1153	  \]
1154	  is \emph{\(J\)-locally injective}. No flatness and no local-triviality
1155	  hypothesis on \(F\) is required.
1156	
1157	  This is the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\)-field content (the injectivity half) of the
1158	  instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}), the load-bearing
1159	  field of the \emph{full-generality} route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path
1160	  (arbitrary \(F\)). For the relative Picard group law, however, the whisker object
1161	  \(F\) supplied by the sole consumer --- the locally-trivial-scoped associator
1162	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- is always \emph{locally trivial}
1163	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), and in that case local injectivity
1164	  of \(F \triangleleft g\) admits a markedly simpler, stalk-free proof, given as the
1165	  PRIMARY route below. The general stalkwise argument (ingredients (d.1)/(d.2)) is
1166	  retained only as the FALLBACK, required if the full route~(e) monoidal category on
1167	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) is later wanted; the locally-trivial
1168	  primary route already suffices for the group law.
1169	\end{lemma}

1171	\begin{proof}
1172	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map,
1173	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}
1174	
1175	  \emph{PRIMARY route (\(F\) locally trivial --- the consumer's case; sheaf-level,
1176	  no stalks, no (d.2)).} Suppose \(F\) is locally trivial
1177	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,F\)), which is exactly the hypothesis its
1178	  sole consumer --- the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} --- supplies.
1179	  \(J\)-local injectivity of a morphism is \emph{local on a covering family}: it
1180	  suffices to produce a cover of \(X\) on each member of which \(F \triangleleft g\)
1181	  restricts to a locally injective morphism. Take a trivialising open cover
1182	  \(\{V\}\) of \(X\) for \(F\), so that \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) on each \(V\).
1183	  On such a \(V\):
1184	  \begin{itemize}
1185	    \item the substrate--restriction compatibility \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1186	      gives a canonical isomorphism
1187	      \[
1188	        (F \triangleleft g)\big|_V \;\cong\; (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)
1189	      \]
1190	      --- restriction along the open immersion \(V \hookrightarrow X\) commutes with
1191	      \(\otimes\), and the whiskering \(F \triangleleft g = \mathrm{id}_F \otimes g\)
1192	      is \(\otimes\) with an identity, so it restricts to \(\mathrm{id}_{F|_V}
1193	      \otimes (g|_V) = (F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V)\);
1194	    \item the trivialisation \(F|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) together with the left
1195	      unitor \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_V \otimes_V (-) \cong
1196	      (-)\)) identifies \((F|_V) \triangleleft (g|_V) \cong g|_V\).
1197	  \end{itemize}
1198	  Composing, \((F \triangleleft g)|_V\) is isomorphic to \(g|_V\). Now \(g \in J.W\),
1199	  and membership in \(J.W\) (local bijectivity) is stable under restriction along an
1200	  open immersion, so \(g|_V \in J.W\); in particular \(g|_V\) is \(J\)-locally
1201	  injective. An isomorphism preserves local injectivity, hence \((F \triangleleft
1202	  g)|_V\) is locally injective on \(V\). As the \(\{V\}\) cover \(X\) and local
1203	  injectivity is local on the cover, \(F \triangleleft g\) is \(J\)-locally
1204	  injective. \emph{No stalks and no (d.2) stalk--tensor commutation enter}; the
1205	  single comparison lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (a presheaf-level
1206	  comparison, decomposed into its two open-immersion base-change halves) carries the
1207	  argument, and thereby moves \emph{onto} the critical path.

1214	
1215	  \emph{FALLBACK route (arbitrary \(F\); stalkwise, required only for the
1216	  full-generality route~(e) \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) path).} For an
1217	  \emph{arbitrary} (not necessarily locally trivial) \(F\), local injectivity is
1218	  proved stalkwise, by the argument Mathlib uses for fixed-base presheaves in
1219	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\)
1220	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}), ported to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\). A
1221	  morphism in \(J.W\) is, on the topological site of \(X\), a \emph{stalkwise
1222	  isomorphism}: at each point \(x\) the induced map on stalks \(g_x\) is an
1223	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1224	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1225	  \[
1226	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1227	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1228	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1229	  \]
1230	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1231	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1232	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1233	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1234	
1235	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1236	  pinned commit:
1237	  \begin{enumerate}
1238	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1239	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1240	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1241	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1242	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1243	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1244	      above.
1245	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1246	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1247	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1248	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1249	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1250	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1251	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1252	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1253	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1254	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1255	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1256	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1257	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1258	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1259	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1260	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1261	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1262	  \end{enumerate}
1263	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1264	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1265	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1266	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1267	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1268	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1269	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.
1270	
1271	  This stalkwise fallback is required \emph{only} if the full-generality
1272	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1273	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1274	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1275	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1276	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1277	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.
1278	\end{proof}

1281	supplies the two whiskering-stability fields of the abandoned
1282	\((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance (\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}). The group law
1283	needs no such instance: the associator is built directly by gluing canonical local
1284	isomorphisms (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}).

1288	\begin{lemma}
1289	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1290	[Flatness-free whiskering preserves the sheafification localizer]
1291	  \label{lem:whisker_of_W}
1292	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1293	  With notation as in \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}: for an
1294	  \emph{arbitrary} presheaf of \(R\)-modules \(F\) and a morphism \(g\) whose
1295	  underlying additive-presheaf map lies in \(J.W\), both whiskerings again lie in
1296	  \(J.W\):
1297	  \[
1298	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; F \triangleleft g \in J.W,
1299	    \qquad
1300	    g \in J.W \;\Longrightarrow\; g \triangleright F \in J.W .
1301	  \]
1302	  These two statements are precisely the \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) and
1303	  \(\mathtt{whiskerRight}\) data required by the class
1304	  \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) for
1305	  the morphism property \(J.W\); together they furnish the instance
1306	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) of \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal}. No flatness and no
1307	  local triviality is used --- they are the flatness-free replacements for the
1308	  flat variants of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer}.
1309	\end{lemma}
1310	
1311	\begin{proof}
1312	  \uses{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}
1313	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of \(J.W\), it suffices to show
1314	  \(F \triangleleft g\) is both locally injective and locally surjective.
1315	  \emph{Local surjectivity} is free and needs no hypothesis on \(F\): the presheaf
1316	  tensor is right exact, so \(F \otimes^{p} -\) carries the locally-zero cokernel
1317	  of \(g\) to the cokernel of \(F \triangleleft g\), which is therefore locally
1318	  zero (Mathlib \(\mathtt{isLocallySurjective\_whiskerLeft}\)). \emph{Local
1319	  injectivity} is \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. The two together
1320	  give \(F \triangleleft g \in J.W\). The right-whiskered statement
1321	  \(g \triangleright F\) follows by conjugating \(F \triangleleft g\) with the
1322	  braiding of the symmetric presheaf monoidal structure, which is an isomorphism
1323	  and hence preserves membership in \(J.W\).
1324	\end{proof}

1336	\begin{lemma}
1337	% SUPERSEDED route (route-(d)/(e) whiskering/stalk machinery); pending deletion once the assoc re-route (morphism-level descent) lands. Declaration still present and still backing the current `tensorObj_assoc_iso` proof.
1338	[The \(J.W\)-monoidality instance and the localized monoidal substrate (route~(e) target)]
1339	  \label{lem:jw_ismonoidal}
1340	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:whisker_of_W}
1341	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The sheafification localizer \(J.W\) on
1342	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\) is \emph{monoidal}: it carries an
1343	  instance
1344	  \[
1345	    J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal},
1346	  \]
1347	  whose two fields are the whiskering-stability lemmas \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}
1348	  (and which is multiplicative because sheafification inverts identities and
1349	  composites). Consequently, by Mathlib's
1350	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)
1351	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}) applied to the already-monoidal category
1352	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\), the localization functor
1353	  \(L = \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.sheafification}\) (which is the localization at
1354	  \(J.W\)), and the unit isomorphism \(L(\mathbf{1}) \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), the
1355	  category
1356	  \[
1357	    \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
1358	      \;=\; \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1359	  \]
1360	  acquires a full \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure whose tensor is the substrate
1361	  \(\otimes_X\) (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}) and whose associator,
1362	  left/right unitors, braiding and \emph{all} coherence (pentagon, triangle,
1363	  naturalities) are derived by the API. This is the route~(e) target: the
1364	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unitors
1365	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} and the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso} are
1366	  then components of this derived monoidal structure rather than hand-assembled
1367	  composites.
1368	
1369	  This block states the route~(e) construction; it depends on the sole open obligation
1370	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}. It mirrors Mathlib's
1371	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) construction one categorical level up.
1372	\end{lemma}
1373	
1374	\begin{proof}
1375	  \uses{lem:whisker_of_W}
1376	  Multiplicativity of \(J.W\) (closure under identities and composition) is part
1377	  of its being a localizer (\(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\)). The two
1378	  whiskering-stability fields of \(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) are
1379	  exactly the left- and right-whiskering statements of \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}.
1380	  Hence \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) holds. Sheafification of presheaves of
1381	  modules is a localization at \(J.W\) (the underlying identification of
1382	  sheafification with localization at the locally-bijective class), and the unit
1383	  presheaf sheafifies to \(\mathcal{O}_X\); feeding these to
1384	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) produces the \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
1385	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with all coherence derived, as in
1386	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}. No \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}\) structure enters
1387	  at any point.
1388	\end{proof}

1390	\begin{lemma}
1391	
1392	\leanok
1393	[Associator for $\otimes_X$ on locally trivial objects]
1394	  \label{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
1395	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_assoc_iso}
1396	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1397	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1398	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1399	  \[
1400	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1401	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1402	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1403	  \]
1404	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1405	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1406	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1407	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1408	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1409	  ever required.
1410	
1411	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1412	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1413	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for
1414	  arbitrary \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; the hypotheses are retained only to match
1415	  the carrier the group law multiplies on, and are not used in the gluing.
1416	\end{lemma}
1417	
1418	\begin{proof}
1419	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1420	  \textbf{Status (route mismatch, deferred).} The \emph{current} realization of this
1421	  associator uses the route-(d) whiskering composite
1422	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} and the sheafification
1423	  bridge \cref{lem:isiso_sheafification_map_of_W}); through
1424	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} --- whose left-whiskering component is
1425	  itself unproved --- it transitively depends on an open proof obligation and is
1426	  therefore \emph{not} axiom-clean. The gluing route described below is the
1427	  \emph{intended} obligation-free realization, but discharging it requires
1428	  \emph{morphism-level descent for \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\)}: gluing a family of
1429	  local isomorphisms, agreeing on overlaps, into a single global isomorphism of
1430	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. That is the same descent-infrastructure family
1431	  the \(\otimes\)-inverse needs (\cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}); accordingly, the
1432	  associator re-route \emph{and} the deletion of the vestigial whiskering/stalk
1433	  apparatus are deferred jointly with the dual block, and are not available with the
1434	  current infrastructure. The gluing description below records the target realization.
1435	
1436	  The isomorphism is built by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through the
1437	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, with no
1438	  recourse to any whiskering or stalk apparatus. Fix a
1439	  common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\). On each member \(U\) form the canonical
1440	  composite
1441	  \[
1442	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1443	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1444	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1445	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1446	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1447	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1448	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1449	  \]
1450	  in which:
1451	  \begin{itemize}
1452	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1453	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1454	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1455	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1456	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1457	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1458	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1459	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1460	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1461	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1462	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1463	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1464	  \end{itemize}
1465	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1466	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1467	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1468	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1469	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1470	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1471	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1472	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1473	  composites agree on the overlap.
1474	
1475	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1476	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1477	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1478	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1479	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1480	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1481	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1482	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1483	  associator. The single non-formal input of this re-route is the
1484	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf
1485	  associator (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules
1486	  internal-hom gluing are already available.
1487	
1488	  Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1489	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1490	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1491	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1492	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1493	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1494	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1495	  are not consumed; the comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is applied to the
1496	  global, untrivialised restrictions \((M \otimes_X N)|_U\), so no free-cover
1497	  specialisation can replace it. The vestigial
1498	  whiskering / \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk apparatus
1499	  (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W,lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W,lem:jw_ismonoidal}) plays
1500	  no part in this proof and is superseded. The group law on \(\otimes\)-iso-classes
1501	  consumes only the \emph{existence} of the resulting isomorphism, never its pentagon,
1502	  triangle, or naturality coherence.
1503	\end{proof}

1593	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
1594	  % corresponding to tensor product.
1595	  % \end{definition}"
1596	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag~01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1597	  invertible} (item~2, the dual of an invertible is invertible and the contraction
1598	  is an isomorphism) and Definition~\texttt{definition-pic} (the Picard group is
1599	  the abelian group of iso-classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules under
1600	  tensor product).}
1601	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
1602	  bundle. The dual
1603	  \[
1604	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1605	  \]
1606	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
1607	  isomorphism
1608	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
1609	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
1610	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
1611	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
1612	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1613	\end{lemma}
1614	
1615	\begin{proof}
1616	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso,
1617	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso, lem:internal_hom_eval, lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}
1618	  \textbf{Infrastructure-blocked.} This statement is \emph{not} realizable with the
1619	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module infrastructure presently available: its construction
1620	  depends on a \emph{sheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules}
1621	  \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\), an object that does not yet
1622	  exist at the presheaf, sheaf, or general-categorical level (there is no
1623	  monoidal-closed / internal-hom / dual structure on
1624	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) or \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), and no object-level
1625	  descent assembling a global \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module from local pieces). The dual
1626	  object \(L^{-1}\) therefore cannot even be \emph{named} until that infrastructure
1627	  is built. The construction of the required internal hom, its dual, and its
1628	  evaluation is decomposed into individually-formalizable sub-steps in
1629	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} (\cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} through
1630	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}); the present lemma is realized once those land,
1631	  and its Lean body is a placeholder until then.
1632	
1633	  We record the intended mathematical route, all three steps of which are correct
1634	  modulo the missing object. \emph{The route is as follows.} Take
1635	  \(L^{-1} := \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) to be the
1636	  sheaf-level dual of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}, and let
1637	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \to \mathcal{O}_X\) be the evaluation of
1638	  \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval}.
1639	
1640	  \emph{Step 1 (\(L^{-1}\) is a line bundle).} Local triviality of \(L\)
1641	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\)) furnishes an open cover \(\{U\}\) of
1642	  \(X\) with trivialisations \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The internal hom commutes
1643	  with restriction along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow X\), so on each \(U\)
1644	  \[
1645	    L^{-1}\big|_U \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(L|_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1646	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U)
1647	                 \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U ,
1648	  \]
1649	  the last identification being the evaluation-at-\(1\) isomorphism for the dual of a
1650	  free rank-one module. Hence \(L^{-1}\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an
1651	  object of \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
1652	
1653	  \emph{Step 2 (the contraction is a local isomorphism).} The evaluation/contraction
1654	  morphism
1655	  \[
1656	    \varepsilon_L \;\colon\; L \otimes_X L^{-1}
1657	      \;=\; L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
1658	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_X,
1659	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
1660	  \]
1661	  is a morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Restricting to a trivialising
1662	  open \(U\), the restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1663	  commutes \(\otimes_X\) past \((-)|_U\):
1664	  \[
1665	    (L \otimes_X L^{-1})\big|_U \;\cong\; L|_U \otimes_U L^{-1}|_U
1666	      \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U ,
1667	  \]
1668	  using the trivialisations of Step~1. Under these identifications \(\varepsilon_L|_U\)
1669	  becomes the contraction \(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U \mathcal{O}_U \to \mathcal{O}_U\) of
1670	  the free rank-one module with its dual, which is precisely the left unitor
1671	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso} (\(\mathcal{O}_U \otimes_U (-) \cong (-)\)) and hence an
1672	  isomorphism. Thus \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) is an isomorphism on each member of the cover.
1673	
1674	  \emph{Step 3 (glue to a global isomorphism).} The local trivialisations are induced
1675	  by the open immersions with no choices, so the local contraction isomorphisms
1676	  \(\varepsilon_L|_U\) agree on the overlaps \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1677	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the unitor under further restriction. As
1678	  ``being an isomorphism'' is local on \(X\) --- a morphism of sheaves of
1679	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules whose restriction to every member of an open cover is an
1680	  isomorphism is itself an isomorphism --- the globally-defined \(\varepsilon_L\) is an
1681	  isomorphism \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\). Equivalently, the
1682	  local inverses glue (they agree on overlaps for the same reason) to a two-sided
1683	  inverse of \(\varepsilon_L\).
1684	
1685	  Consequently, \emph{once \(L^{-1}\) and \(\varepsilon_L\) are available from
1686	  \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}}, every line bundle \(L\) has the two-sided tensor
1687	  inverse \(L^{-1}\) under \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes of
1688	  line bundles is an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
1689	  The local-triviality of \(L^{-1}\) (Step~1) is \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} and
1690	  the local-iso\(\Rightarrow\)global-iso passage (Step~3) is the already-closed
1691	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the single non-available input is the dual object
1692	  and its evaluation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
1693	\end{proof}

1695	\begin{lemma}
1696	
1697	\leanok
1698	[Closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial carrier
1699	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
1700	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1701	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObjOnProduct}
1702	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj,
1703	        lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1704	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes. The carrier is the
1705	  \emph{locally-trivial} subtype
1706	  \[
1707	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1708	    \;\;=\;\;
1709	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
1710	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}
1711	  \]
1712	  introduced in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. The bifunctor
1713	  \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
1714	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to this subtype:
1715	  if \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M\) and \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M'\) then
1716	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}(M \otimes_{C \times_S T} M')\). Hence \(\otimes\)
1717	  closes on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\), supplying the binary
1718	  operation the relative group law multiplies with.
1719	\end{lemma}
1720	
1721	\begin{proof}
1722	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
1723	  Closure is exactly
1724	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}: the tensor product of two
1725	  locally-trivial rank-one modules is again locally trivial of rank one
1726	  (\(\mathtt{tensorObj\_isLocallyTrivial}\)). The Lean construction
1727	  \(\mathtt{tensorObjOnProduct}\) applies this directly to a pair of objects of
1728	  the \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) subtype \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\),
1729	  returning the tensor with its locally-trivial witness; no
1730	  \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate and no coherence isomorphism enters this
1731	  closure statement. (The \(\otimes\)-invertibility carrier
1732	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible} is a
1733	  separate predicate, connected to \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) only by an
1734	  off-critical-path equivalence; the units-group packaging is
1735	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, not this lemma.)
1736	\end{proof}
1737	
1738	\begin{lemma}
1739	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
1740	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
1741	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle.OnProduct.pullback_tensorObj_iso}
1742	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1743	  % NOTE: pullback preserves the structure sheaf and tensor products (and hence
1744	  % tensor-invertibility) by the standard pullback-tensor compatibility of
1745	  % \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules (Stacks \texttt{lemma-tensor-product-pullback} /
1746	  % \texttt{lemma-pullback-invertible}), independently of the open-immersion
1747	  % restriction-compatibility iso.
1748	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
1749	  functor
1750	  \[
1751	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
1752	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
1753	  \]
1754	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
1755	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
1756	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
1757	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
1758	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
1759	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
1760	  canonical isomorphism
1761	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
1762	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
1763	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
1764	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
1765	\end{lemma}
1766	
1767	\begin{proof}
1768	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
1769	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
1770	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
1771	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
1772	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
1773	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
1774	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
1775	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\): on each
1776	  affine open where \(\mathcal{N}\) and \(\mathcal{N}'\) are free of rank one
1777	  over \(A\), both sides evaluate to the free rank-one \(B\)-module, so
1778	  the comparison is an isomorphism. No flatness of \(A \to B\) is required
1779	  at this level, as the modules involved are locally free. The compatibility
1780	  with the unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
1781	  same cover.
1782	\end{proof}

1784	\section{\(\otimes\)-invertibility and the commutative-monoid group-law engine}
1785	\label{sec:tensorobj_invertibility}
1786	
1787	The relative Picard group law is assembled from \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertibility}
1788	and the monoidal coherence isomorphisms of
1789	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1790	following Mathlib's Picard-group idiom (\(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\),
1791	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic} = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\),
1792	\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\); \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) as the
1793	design template. Under the route~(e) fallback the inverse of an invertible object
1794	is carried by the predicate itself, so neither the open-immersion
1795	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} nor a
1796	separate tensor-inverse lemma is needed there. Under the PRIMARY locally-trivial
1797	route, by contrast, both \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (via the PRIMARY proof
1798	of \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}) and the tensor-inverse lemma
1799	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (supplying inverses to
1800	\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) are on the critical path.

1848	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). We say \(M\)
1849	  is \emph{\(\otimes\)-invertible} when it admits a tensor inverse: there is an
1850	  object \(N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) together with an isomorphism
1851	  \[
1852	    M \otimes_X N \;\cong\;
1853	      \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}
1854	      \;=\; \mathcal{O}_X ,
1855	  \]

1857	  \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong
1858	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\bigr)\), with
1859	  \(\otimes_X\) the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The
1860	  predicate is a \emph{proposition} carrying its inverse witness existentially;
1861	  consequently the dual/inverse needed by the group law is definitional and no
1862	  separate ``a tensor inverse exists'' lemma is consumed downstream. This is the
1863	  scheme-level analogue of Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\), transported
1864	  one categorical level up to \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with the substrate
1865	  \(\otimes_X\).

1917	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR (Modules on Ringed Spaces,
1918	  ``Invertible modules''), Definition~01CX and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-%
1919	  invertible}. The isomorphism classes of invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
1920	  form an abelian group \(\Pic(X)\) under \(\otimes\), with unit
1921	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\) and inverse the dual; the tensor product of invertibles is
1922	  invertible and the dual is a tensor inverse.}
1923	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The isomorphism classes of \(\otimes\)-invertible
1924	  objects of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
1925	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}) form a commutative monoid under the
1926	  substrate tensor product \(\otimes_X\) in which every element is a unit ---
1927	  equivalently, an abelian group --- the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag
1928	  01CR).
1929	
1930	  \textbf{Primary construction (directly on locally-trivial iso-classes, à la
1931	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\)).} The commutative group is built directly on
1932	  isomorphism classes of \emph{locally-trivial} (equivalently \(\otimes\)-invertible)
1933	  sheaves, mirroring Mathlib's ring-level Picard group
1934	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
1935	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The group operation is
1936	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\), well-defined on iso-classes by the
1937	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
1938	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) and again locally trivial by
1939	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}; the identity is
1940	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\); and the inverse of \([L]\) is the class of the dual
1941	  \([L^{-1}]\) supplied by \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} (with
1942	  \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)).
1943	
1944	  The four group-axiom fields are defined \emph{by hand}, each discharged by a
1945	  \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism read as an equality of iso-classes --- passage
1946	  to iso-classes turns an isomorphism of sheaves into an equality of classes:
1947	  \[
1948	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \lambda_X \rangle,
1949	    \quad
1950	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \rho_X \rangle,
1951	    \quad
1952	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \alpha_{X,Y,Z} \rangle,
1953	    \quad
1954	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \;\leftarrow\; \langle \beta_{X,Y} \rangle,
1955	  \]
1956	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity is
1957	  the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
1958	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, each consumed only as the proposition
1959	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\); the pentagon, triangle and hexagon
1960	  coherence carried by a \(\MonoidalCategory\) typeclass is \emph{never} invoked in
1961	  any of the four axiom proofs.
1962	
1963	  This by-hand assembly does \emph{not} go through
1964	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\)
1965	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}): those constructions
1966	  \emph{require} a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\) (and, for the commutative
1967	  law, \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on the carrier category --- precisely the
1968	  coherent monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) over the \emph{varying}
1969	  structure sheaf that this construction explicitly avoids. Consequently the group law
1970	  requires \emph{no} \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \emph{no}
1971	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) and \emph{no} \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\) instance on
1972	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The genuine precedent is Mathlib's fixed-ring
1973	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R := \mathrm{Skeleton}(
1974	  \mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\) (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}):
1975	  there the \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) \emph{is} read off the \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) of the
1976	  coherent monoidal category \(\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\,R)\)
1977	  that Mathlib supplies over a fixed ring. The present construction \emph{cannot} take
1978	  that \(\mathrm{Skeleton}\) route --- no coherent monoidal category on
1979	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) for the varying structure sheaf is available --- so
1980	  its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is built directly from the existence-of-isomorphism
1981	  propositions above, the construction ultimately resting, as
1982	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) does, on iso-classes of invertible modules. The pinned
1983	  carrier \(\mathtt{tensorObjIsoclassCommMonoid}\) realizes this commutative-group
1984	  structure; the scheme-level construction is genuinely absent from Mathlib at the
1985	  pinned commit.
1986	
1987	  This is the \emph{lower-risk} path: every ingredient is either already proved
1988	  (the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the braiding
1989	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}) or locally-trivial-scoped and assembled (the
1990	  associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}). The only remaining obligations on this
1991	  critical path are the restriction-compatibility comparison
1992	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (which the primary whisker route of
1993	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} also now consumes) and the
1994	  dual/inverse \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. In particular the group law
1995	  never invokes the stalk--tensor commutation (d.2) over a varying ring.
1996	
1997	  \textbf{Fallback (full route~(e)).} Alternatively, once
1998	  \cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} lands, \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X =
1999	  \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) is a full (symmetric)
2000	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) via \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\),
2001	  and the Picard group is the units of its skeleton,
2002	  \(\Pic(X) = \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\),
2003	  exactly as Mathlib builds \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R =
2004	  \mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) and transports
2005	  \(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\). This fallback yields the group on \emph{all}
2006	  invertible modules and is retained for the full monoidal generality, but it is
2007	  not required for the relative Picard group law, which the primary construction
2008	  already supplies.
2009	\end{lemma}

2013	        lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality, lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2014	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,

2017	  \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) / \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)
2018	  (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/PicardGroup.lean}). The binary operation
2019	  \([L]\cdot[M] := [L \otimes_X M]\) is well-defined on iso-classes by the
2020	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2021	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}) --- an isomorphism in either
2022	  argument tensors to an isomorphism --- and its value is again locally trivial by
2023	  the closure lemma \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, so the operation closes on
2024	  the carrier. The identity element is \([\mathcal{O}_X]\). The four group-axiom
2025	  fields are each defined \emph{by hand} from a \emph{single} existence-of-isomorphism,
2026	  read as an equality of iso-classes,
2027	  \[
2028	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle\alpha\rangle,
2029	    \quad
2030	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle\lambda\rangle,
2031	    \quad
2032	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle\rho\rangle,
2033	    \quad
2034	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle\beta\rangle:
2035	  \]
2036	  associativity is \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, the unit laws are the unitors
2037	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, and commutativity is the braiding
2038	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}. No pentagon, triangle or hexagon identity is
2039	  invoked, since each law asserts only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
2040	  This is the same one-isomorphism-per-law shape that Mathlib's
2041	  \(\mathtt{monoidOfSkeletalMonoidal}\)
2042	  (\texttt{Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Skeleton.lean}) uses on a skeletal
2043	  monoidal category via its skeletality map \(\mathtt{hC}\), but we do \emph{not}
2044	  instantiate that construction: it requires a coherent \([\mathtt{MonoidalCategory}]\)
2045	  (and \([\mathtt{BraidedCategory}]\)) on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), which is
2046	  exactly the structure for the varying structure sheaf that is unavailable.

2048	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} satisfies \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong
2049	  \mathcal{O}_X\), so \([L^{-1}]\) is a two-sided inverse of \([L]\); equivalently,
2050	  \([L]\) is a unit precisely when \(L\) is \(\otimes\)-invertible
2051	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse witness carried
2052	  existentially by \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\). This assembles the abelian group ---
2053	  the Picard group of \(X\) (Stacks tag 01CR) --- with \emph{no}
2054	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) or \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2055	  exactly as \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) carries its \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) on
2056	  iso-classes of invertible modules (there off a fixed-ring \(\mathtt{Skeleton}\),
2057	  here by hand).

2060	  \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) via
2061	  \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\), the group is the units of the skeleton,
2062	  \(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X))\), exactly the
2063	  Mathlib \(\mathtt{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\cdots))\) template used for
2064	  \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\); this route is not needed for the group law.

2067	\section{The invertibility-carrier Picard group}
2068	\label{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}
2069	
2070	The group law of \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup} is carried on the
2071	\emph{locally-trivial} predicate \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\), and on that carrier
2072	the existence of a tensor inverse for each class is an extra obligation
2073	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}): one must \emph{manufacture} the dual
2074	object \(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\) and prove the
2075	contraction \(L \otimes_X L^{-1} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), which is the whole content of
2076	the dual-infrastructure build of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}.
2077	
2078	This section carries the group law on a \emph{different} predicate for which that
2079	obligation evaporates: the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
2080	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
2081	\[
2082	  \mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)
2083	  \;:\equiv\;
2084	  \exists\, N,\ \mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\bigr).
2085	\]
2086	On this carrier the inverse of a class \([M]\) is supplied by the \emph{membership
2087	witness itself} --- the very \(N\) whose existence \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}(M)\)
2088	asserts --- so no inverse object need be constructed and no separate
2089	tensor-inverse lemma is consumed. The cost is shifted to a routine
2090	well-definedness fact (\cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef}: the witness \(N\) is
2091	determined up to isomorphism), proved from the monoidal coherence isomorphisms
2092	alone. The carrier is the scheme-theoretic analogue of Mathlib's
2093	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\), whose elements are exactly the iso-classes of invertible
2094	modules (Stacks tag 01CX).

2106	  % SOURCE QUOTE:

2115	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}: a locally free rank-one
2116	  module is invertible, and over a space whose stalks are local rings (in

2120	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be \(\otimes\)-invertible
2121	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}). There is an isomorphism
2122	  \[
2123	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
2124	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2125	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
2126	  \]

2128	  (\cref{thm:pic_commgroup}) consumes only the \emph{existence} of this
2129	  isomorphism, the proposition \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P

2134	\begin{proof}
2135	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2136	        lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso, lem:flat_whisker_localizer}
2137	  On a scheme \(X\) every stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,x}\) is a local ring, so by Stacks
2138	  tag 0B8M an \(\otimes\)-invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is \emph{locally free
2139	  of rank one}, and a locally free module is in particular \emph{flat}: each
2140	  section \(M(U)\) is a flat \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module. This flatness is the key
2141	  difference from the general-module associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}.
2142	
2143	  \emph{Routing past the open obligation.} The general-module associator
2144	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is realized through the route-(d) whiskering
2145	  composite, whose left-whiskering half is the flatness-free localizer lemma
2146	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (the variants
2147	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\) / \(\mathtt{isLocallyInjective\_whiskerLeft\_of\_W}\)).
2148	  That half is an \emph{open} obligation, so \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} is
2149	  sorry-transitive. For \emph{invertible} \(M, N, P\) the whiskering objects are
2150	  flat, so the whiskering-stability needed by the associator is instead supplied by
2151	  the \emph{flat}-whiskering lemma \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (the variants
2152	  \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerLeft\_of\_flat}\) / \(\mathtt{W\_whiskerRight\_of\_flat}\)),
2153	  which is sorry-clean: there local injectivity of \(F \triangleleft g\) follows
2154	  from \(F(U)\) flat over \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\) preserving injective linear maps
2155	  (\(\mathtt{Module.Flat.lTensor\_preserves\_injective\_linearMap}\)), with no open
2156	  residual. Restricting the construction of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} to
2157	  invertible arguments therefore bypasses the flatness-free open obligation of
2158	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}.
2159	
2160	  The remaining ingredients are exactly those of \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}: the
2161	  three canonical arrows of the local composite are the restriction-compatibility
2162	  comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (twice, on each member of a common
2163	  open cover and in reverse) framing the canonical presheaf-level associator of
2164	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\). Each arrow is canonical, hence
2165	  natural under further restriction, so the local composites agree on double
2166	  overlaps and glue to the global associator. The invertibility hypotheses are used
2167	  only to route through the flat-whiskering lemma; the gluing itself is unchanged.
2168	\end{proof}
