2337	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CR, Lemma~\texttt{lemma-invertible}: when
2338	  \(\mathcal{L}\) is invertible the module \(\mathcal{N}\) with
2339	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N} \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) is
2340	  isomorphic to the dual \(\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)\),
2341	  hence unique up to isomorphism.}
2342	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(M, N, N' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). If
2343	  \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) and \(M \otimes_X N' \cong \mathcal{O}_X\),
2344	  then \(N \cong N'\). Consequently the assignment \([M] \mapsto [N]\), sending an
2345	  invertible class to the class of its tensor inverse, is well-defined on
2346	  \(\Pic X\).
2347	\end{lemma}
2348	
2349	\begin{proof}
2350	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2351	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible, lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}
2352	  This is the standard ``inverse-of-inverse'' argument in a commutative monoid, run
2353	  with isomorphisms in place of equalities. Chain the unitor
2354	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, the two hypotheses, the associator
2355	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible} and the braiding
2356	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}:
2357	  \[
2358	    N
2359	    \;\cong\; N \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X
2360	    \;\cong\; N \otimes_X (M \otimes_X N')
2361	    \;\cong\; (N \otimes_X M) \otimes_X N'
2362	    \;\cong\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X N'
2363	    \;\cong\; N',
2364	  \]
2365	  where the second step substitutes \(\mathcal{O}_X \cong M \otimes_X N'\), the
2366	  third is the associator, the fourth uses the braiding
2367	  \(N \otimes_X M \cong M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\), and the outer two are
2368	  the unitors. Hence \(N \cong N'\). For well-definedness on classes, note that if
2369	  \(M \cong M'\) then a tensor inverse of \(M\) is also a tensor inverse of \(M'\)
2370	  (tensor with the isomorphism), so the inverse class depends only on \([M]\).
2371	\end{proof}
2372	
2373	\begin{theorem}
2374	[The invertibility-carrier Picard group is abelian]
2375	  \label{thm:pic_commgroup}
2376	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.picCommGroup}
2377	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
2378	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2379	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2380	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2381	  % modules), Definition definition-pic + Lemma lemma-constructions-invertible
2382	  % (the iso-classes of invertibles form an abelian group under tensor product)
2383	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4200-L4213 and L4350-L4357).
2384	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2385	  % "\begin{lemma}
2386	  % \label{lemma-constructions-invertible}
2387	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2388	  % \begin{enumerate}
2389	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$, $\mathcal{N}$ are invertible
2390	  % $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, then so is
2391	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X} \mathcal{N}$.
2392	  % \item If $\mathcal{L}$ is an invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-module, then so is
2393	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X)$ and the evaluation map
2394	  % $\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2395	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X$
2396	  % is an isomorphism.
2397	  % \end{enumerate}
2398	  % \end{lemma}
2399	  % [\dots]
2400	  % \begin{definition}
2401	  % \label{definition-pic}
2402	  % Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2403	  % The {\it Picard group} $\Pic(X)$ of $X$ is the
2404	  % abelian group whose elements are isomorphism classes of
2405	  % invertible $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules, with addition
2406	  % corresponding to tensor product.
2407	  % \end{definition}"
2408	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01CX, Definition~\texttt{definition-pic}
2409	  and Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible}: the isomorphism classes of
2410	  invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules form an abelian group under tensor product,
2411	  with unit \(\mathcal{O}_X\).}
2412	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The carrier \(\Pic X\) of \cref{def:pic_carrier} is an
2413	  abelian group, with
2414	  \[
2415	    [M] \cdot [M'] := [M \otimes_X M'],
2416	    \qquad
2417	    1 := [\mathcal{O}_X],
2418	    \qquad
2419	    [M]^{-1} := [N] \ \text{for any witness } M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X.
2420	  \]
2421	\end{theorem}
2422	
2423	\begin{proof}
2424	  \uses{def:pic_carrier, lem:isinvertible_tensor, lem:isinvertible_unit,
2425	        lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef, lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2426	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso, lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible}
2427	  Each group-operation datum and each axiom is supplied by a single
2428	  existence-of-isomorphism, read as an equality of classes in \(\Pic X\).
2429	
2430	  \emph{The operations are well-defined.} The product \([M] \cdot [M'] := [M
2431	  \otimes_X M']\) lands in \(\Pic X\) because \(\otimes_X\) preserves invertibility
2432	  (\cref{lem:isinvertible_tensor}), and it is independent of representatives by the
2433	  bifunctoriality of \(\otimes_X\)
2434	  (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}): an isomorphism in either
2435	  argument tensors to an isomorphism, hence an equality of classes. The unit
2436	  \([\mathcal{O}_X]\) lies in \(\Pic X\) by \cref{lem:isinvertible_unit}. The
2437	  inverse \([M] \mapsto [N]\), \(N\) any membership witness of
2438	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\,M\), is well-defined by
2439	  \cref{lem:isinvertible_inverse_welldef} --- the witness is determined up to
2440	  isomorphism, so its class depends only on \([M]\). Crucially, \emph{no inverse
2441	  object is constructed}: the inverse is read off the existential carried by
2442	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\).
2443	
2444	  \emph{The axioms.} Each axiom is the image, under passage to iso-classes, of one
2445	  coherence isomorphism of \(\otimes_X\):
2446	  \[
2447	    \mathtt{one\_mul} \leftarrow \langle \lambda \rangle,
2448	    \quad
2449	    \mathtt{mul\_one} \leftarrow \langle \rho \rangle,
2450	    \quad
2451	    \mathtt{mul\_assoc} \leftarrow \langle \alpha \rangle,
2452	    \quad
2453	    \mathtt{mul\_comm} \leftarrow \langle \beta \rangle,
2454	    \quad
2455	    \mathtt{mul\_left\_inv} \leftarrow \langle \varepsilon \rangle,
2456	  \]
2457	  where the unit laws are the unitors \cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}, associativity
2458	  is the invertible-case associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso_invertible},
2459	  commutativity is the braiding \cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, and the left-inverse
2460	  law \([N] \cdot [M] = [N \otimes_X M] = [\mathcal{O}_X] = 1\) is the witness
2461	  isomorphism \(\varepsilon : N \otimes_X M \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) itself (obtained
2462	  from \(M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\) by the braiding). Each axiom asserts
2463	  only \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\), so no pentagon, triangle, or
2464	  hexagon coherence is invoked; commutativity makes the left-inverse law suffice
2465	  for a two-sided inverse.
2466	
2467	  This is the invertibility-carrier analogue of
2468	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}, differing in exactly one respect: the
2469	  left-inverse field is discharged by the predicate's own membership witness rather
2470	  than by the manufactured dual of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}. The
2471	  whole dual-infrastructure obligation of \cref{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra} is
2472	  thereby off this group's critical path.
2473	\end{proof}
2474	
2475	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
2476	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
2477	
2478	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
2479	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
2480	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
2481	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2482	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
2483	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
2484	
2485	\begin{theorem}
2486	
2487	\leanok
2488	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
2489	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
2490	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
2491	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2492	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2493	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2494	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2495	        lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,
2496	        lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,
2497	        lem:tensorobj_comm_iso,
2498	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2499	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2500	
2501	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
2502	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
2503	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
2504	  % groups)
2505	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
2506	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2507	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
2508	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
2509	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
2510	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
2511	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
2512	  % \end{dfn}
2513	  %  [\dots]
2514	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
2515	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
2516	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
2517	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
2518	  % topologies  by
2519	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
2520	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
2521	  %  \end{dfn}"
2522	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
2523	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
2524	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
2525	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
2526	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
2527	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
2528	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
2529	  \[
2530	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
2531	  \]
2532	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
2533	  abelian-group structure with addition
2534	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
2535	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
2536	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
2537	  inverse
2538	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
2539	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
2540	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2541	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
2542	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
2543	  map
2544	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
2545	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
2546	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2547	\end{theorem}
2548	
2549	\begin{proof}
2550	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
2551	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
2552	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
2553	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
2554	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
2555	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
2556	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
2557	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
2558	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
2559	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
2560	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
2561	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
2562	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
2563	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
2564	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
2565	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
2566	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
2567	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
2568	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
2569	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
2570	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
2571	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
2572	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
2573	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
2574	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
2575	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
2576	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
2577	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
2578	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
2579	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
2580	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
2581	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
2582	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
2583	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
2584	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2585	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
2586	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
2587	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
2588	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
2589	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
2590	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
2591	
2592	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
2593	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
2594	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
2595	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
2596	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
2597	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
2598	
2599	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
2600	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
2601	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
2602	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
2603	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
2604	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
2605	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
2606	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
2607	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
2608	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
2609	\end{proof}

2753	\section{Sheaf internal-hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules (the \(\otimes\)-inverse
2754	  infrastructure)}
2755	\label{sec:tensorobj_dual_infra}
2756	
2757	The PRIMARY group law (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) needs a
2758	\(\otimes\)-inverse for every line bundle, supplied by
2759	\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} as the dual
2760	\(L^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)\). The standard route
2761	constructs that dual as a special case of the \emph{sheaf internal hom} of
2762	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules; over a fixed ring the internal hom of \(R\)-modules is the
2763	linear-coyoneda module \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\), with dual
2764	\(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,\mathbf{1} = M^\vee\), but for the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
2765	no such
2766	internal-hom / monoidal-closed structure exists at the
2767	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\), \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\), or general-categorical
2768	level. This section decomposes the construction of the missing object into
2769	individually-formalizable sub-steps, following the slice presentation of the internal
2770	hom; each block names the intended Lean declaration and its dependencies. The
2771	specialisation that closes \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is recorded in
2772	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse}; an alternative descent-theoretic route is noted in
2773	\cref{rem:dual_via_stack}.
2774	
2775	\emph{Status: deferred, off the group's critical path.} The blocks of this section
2776	--- the dual object \(\mathtt{dual}\) and its restriction compatibility
2777	\cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}, the local-triviality of the dual
2778	\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, the gluing engine
2779	\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}, and the discharge remark
2780	\cref{rem:dual_discharges_inverse} --- together constitute the \emph{deferred}
2781	\(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftrightarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) bridge: on a
2782	scheme an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is locally free of rank one, and
2783	conversely (Stacks tag 0B8M), so the two carriers agree, and the locally-trivial
2784	carrier's missing inverse (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}) is recovered
2785	precisely from the dual built here.
2786	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 0B8M (Modules on Ringed Spaces, \S Invertible
2787	% modules), Lemma lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1
2788	% (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L4159-L4165).
2789	% SOURCE QUOTE:
2790	% "\begin{lemma}
2791	% \label{lemma-invertible-is-locally-free-rank-1}
2792	% Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space. Any locally free
2793	% $\mathcal{O}_X$-module of rank $1$ is invertible.
2794	% If all stalks $\mathcal{O}_{X, x}$ are local rings, then
2795	% the converse holds as well (but in general this is not the case).
2796	% \end{lemma}"
2797	However, the relative Picard \emph{group law} is now carried on the
2798	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
2799	(\cref{sec:tensorobj_pic_carrier}, \cref{thm:pic_commgroup}), where the inverse is
2800	the membership witness and \emph{no} dual object is constructed. This section is
2801	therefore needed only by a downstream consumer that specifically requires the
2802	locally-trivial form of an inverse --- not by the group law. The blocks are retained
2803	intact for that future use.
2804	
2805	A structural point governs the whole construction. The naive rule
2806	\(U \mapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) is \emph{contravariant} in the
2807	restriction maps of \(M\) (a restriction \(M(U) \to M(V)\) for \(V \subseteq U\)
2808	induces precomposition \(\Hom(M(V), N(V)) \to \Hom(M(U), \cdot)\) in the wrong
2809	direction), so it is \emph{not} a presheaf in the covariant sense and cannot be
2810	sheafified like the tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}. The remedy is
2811	the \emph{slice} formula \(U \mapsto (M|_U \to N|_U)\) --- the module of morphisms of
2812	\emph{restricted} modules over the restricted ring --- whose presheaf functoriality
2813	(restrict a global-over-\(U\) morphism to a global-over-\(V\) morphism, for
2814	\(V \subseteq U\)) \emph{is} covariant. Constructing that slice presheaf, and
2815	proving it satisfies the sheaf condition, is the substance of the build.
2816	
2817	\begin{definition}
2818	
2819	\leanok
2820	[The global-ring module on morphisms of presheaves of modules (value module)]
2821	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2822	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.homModule}
2823	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2824	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2825	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2826	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3508--L3514).
2827	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2828	  % "abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2829	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2830	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2831	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2832	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2833	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2834	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
2835	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2836	  (tag area 01CM): the section ring acts on a morphism of restricted modules by
2837	  post-composition with multiplication by the section.}
2838	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on a base category \(\mathcal{C}\)
2839	  that has a terminal object \(T\), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\).
2840	  The abelian group of morphisms \(M \longrightarrow N\) of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
2841	  carries a canonical \(R(T)\)-module structure --- the \emph{morphism module}
2842	  \(\mathtt{homModule}\,M\,N\). A global scalar \(f \in R(T)\) acts on a morphism
2843	  \(\varphi : M \to N\) by post-composition with multiplication by \(f\),
2844	  \[
2845	    f \cdot \varphi \;:=\; m_f^{N} \circ \varphi,
2846	  \]
2847	  where \(m_f^{N} : N \to N\) is the \emph{multiplication-by-\(f\)} endomorphism of
2848	  \(N\): the endomorphism that, on each object \(U \in \mathcal{C}\), is multiplication
2849	  by the restriction \(f|_U \in R(U)\) of \(f\) along the unique morphism \(U \to T\)
2850	  to the terminal object. The module axioms hold because the assignment
2851	  \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\) is a ring homomorphism \(R(T) \to \mathrm{End}(N)\) into the
2852	  endomorphism ring of \(N\) --- a product \(f \cdot g\) goes to the \emph{composite}
2853	  \(m_f^{N} \circ m_g^{N}\) of the two multiplication endomorphisms, the order in which
2854	  the factors compose being reversed by the fact that the scalar acts by
2855	  \emph{post}-composition --- and composition of morphisms is biadditive in the
2856	  preadditive category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). Distributivity and the
2857	  associativity of the action then follow from bilinearity of composition together with
2858	  the homomorphism property of \(f \mapsto m_f^{N}\). This is the genuinely new core of
2859	  the construction: Mathlib supplies the \emph{fixed}-ring internal hom
2860	  \(\mathtt{ihom}\,M\,N = (M \to N)\) over a single base ring, but nothing equipping the
2861	  morphism group with the module structure for the \emph{varying} structure presheaf at
2862	  the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
2863	\end{definition}
2864	
2865	\begin{definition}
2866	
2867	\leanok
2868	[Slice specialisation of the morphism module]
2869	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2870	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHomObjModule}
2871	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
2872	  Specialise \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} to a slice category. For an object
2873	  (open) \(U\) of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\), restricting along the forgetful functor
2874	  \(\mathtt{Over.forget}\,U : \mathcal{C}/U \to \mathcal{C}\) --- the open-immersion
2875	  restriction \(M \mapsto M|_U\) --- carries \(M, N\) to presheaves of modules
2876	  \(M|_U, N|_U\) over the restricted structure ring on the slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\). The
2877	  slice \(\mathcal{C}/U\) has the terminal object \(\mathtt{Over.mk}\,(\mathrm{id}_U)\),
2878	  at which the restricted ring takes the value \(R(U)\); hence
2879	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} makes the morphism group
2880	  \(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\) an \(R(U)\)-module, the
2881	  \(\mathtt{internalHomObjModule}\). This \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) is exactly
2882	  the value \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
2883	\end{definition}
2884	
2885	\begin{definition}
2886	
2887	\leanok
2888	[Presheaf internal hom of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
2889	  \label{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
2890	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.internalHom}
2891	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, def:presheaf_internal_hom_value,
2892	        def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2893	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2894	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2895	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3500--L3524).
2896	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2897	  % "Let $(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ be a ringed space.
2898	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2899	  % Consider the rule
2900	  % $$
2901	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2902	  % $$
2903	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2904	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2905	  % abelian groups. In addition, given an element
2906	  % $\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2907	  % and a section $f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)$ then we can define
2908	  % $f\varphi \in \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)$
2909	  % by either precomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{F}|_U$
2910	  % or postcomposing with multiplication by $f$ on $\mathcal{G}|_U$ (it gives
2911	  % the same result). Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules.
2912	  % We will denote this sheaf
2913	  % $\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})$.
2914	  % There is a canonical ``evaluation'' morphism
2915	  % $$
2916	  % \mathcal{F}
2917	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
2918	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
2919	  % \longrightarrow
2920	  % \mathcal{G}.
2921	  % $$"
2922	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2923	  (tag area 01CM).}
2924	  Let \(R\) be a presheaf of commutative rings on \(\Opens X\) and let
2925	  \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). The \emph{presheaf internal hom}
2926	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules whose value on an open
2927	  \(U\) is the module of morphisms of restricted modules
2928	  \[
2929	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U)
2930	      \;:=\;
2931	    \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\;\bigl(R(U)\bigr)\;\bigl(M|_U \longrightarrow N|_U\bigr),
2932	  \]
2933	  the \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear morphisms \(M|_U \to N|_U\) of presheaves of
2934	  modules on \(U\), formed via the open-immersion restriction \(U \hookrightarrow X\).
2935	  This value module is the slice specialisation
2936	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value} of the morphism module
2937	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}.
2938	
2939	  Explicitly, \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) is the presheaf of \(R\)-modules assembled from
2940	  three pieces. \emph{(a) Value modules.} On each open \(U\) the value is the
2941	  \(R(U)\)-module \((M|_U \to N|_U)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} /
2942	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}. \emph{(b) Restriction maps.} For an
2943	  inclusion \(V \subseteq U\) the restriction map
2944	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \to \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V)\) is the further-restriction map
2945	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}, \(\varphi \mapsto \varphi|_V\), which
2946	  is additive and semilinear over the ring restriction \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\)
2947	  induced by \(g : V \to U\) --- the compatibility datum that the presheaf-of-modules
2948	  assembly (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.mk}\), via \(\mathtt{ofPresheaf}\)) consumes.
2949	  \emph{(c) Functoriality.} Restriction along the identity \(U \to U\) is the identity
2950	  map, and restriction along a composite \(V \xrightarrow{g} U \xrightarrow{h} W\) of
2951	  inclusions \(V \subseteq U \subseteq W\) (the morphism \(g : V \to U\) followed by
2952	  \(h : U \to W\) in \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\)) is the composite of the two
2953	  restriction maps \(\mathcal{H}om(M,N)(W) \to \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(U) \to
2954	  \mathcal{H}om(M,N)(V)\); both identity and composition are inherited from the
2955	  functoriality of further restriction, since \(\mathtt{Over.map}\) is a functor. These three pieces
2956	  make \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) a genuine, covariant presheaf of \(R\)-modules; this
2957	  assembled presheaf is the construction target \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.internalHom}\).
2958	
2959	  The slice formula is forced by contravariance: the pointwise rule
2960	  \(U \mapsto \Hom_{R(U)}(M(U), N(U))\) varies \emph{against} the restriction maps of
2961	  \(M\) and so is not a covariant presheaf, whereas the module of morphisms of
2962	  \emph{restricted} objects \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricts covariantly. Thus, unlike the
2963	  tensor product \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} --- which is covariant in the
2964	  restriction maps and sheafifies directly --- the internal hom must be built through
2965	  the slice presentation, and there is no presheaf-level dual to sheafify naively.
2966	\end{definition}

2968	\begin{lemma}
2969	
2970	\leanok
2971	[Restriction map of the presheaf internal hom]
2972	  \label{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
2973	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.InternalHom.restrictionMap}
2974	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value, def:presheaf_internal_hom_slice_value}
2975	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
2976	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
2977	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3508).
2978	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2979	  % "Consider the rule
2980	  % $$
2981	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
2982	  % $$
2983	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
2984	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
2985	  % abelian groups."
2986	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
2987	  (tag area 01CM): the rule \(U \mapsto \Hom(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U)\), with its
2988	  further-restriction maps, is a (pre)sheaf of abelian groups.}
2989	  Let \(g : V \to U\) be a morphism of \(\mathcal{C} = \Opens X\) (an inclusion
2990	  \(V \subseteq U\) of opens), and let \(M, N \in \mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\). There
2991	  is a restriction map
2992	  \[
2993	    \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(U) \longrightarrow \mathcal{H}om(M, N)(V),
2994	    \qquad \varphi \longmapsto \varphi|_V,
2995	  \]
2996	  the \(\mathtt{restrictionMap}\), sending a morphism of restricted modules
2997	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to its further restriction \(M|_V \to N|_V\). Concretely, the
2998	  functor \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g : \mathcal{C}/V \to \mathcal{C}/U\) realises
2999	  ``restrict from \(U\) to \(V\)'', and \(\varphi|_V\) is \(\varphi\) pulled back along
3000	  it --- the further restriction of the same morphism of presheaves of modules. This
3001	  map is additive, and it is \emph{semilinear} over the ring restriction
3002	  \(R(g) : R(U) \to R(V)\): for \(f \in R(U)\),
3003	  \[
3004	    (f \cdot \varphi)\big|_V \;=\; R(g)(f) \cdot \bigl(\varphi|_V\bigr).
3005	  \]
3006	  This semilinearity is the compatibility datum that the presheaf assembly of
3007	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} (through \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.ofPresheaf}\) /
3008	  \(\mathtt{mk}\)) requires.
3009	\end{lemma}
3010	
3011	\begin{proof}
3012	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value}
3013	  Further restriction along \(\mathtt{Over.map}\,g\) is functorial, so it carries a
3014	  morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\) to a morphism \(M|_V \to N|_V\) and respects identities and
3015	  composites; in particular the induced map on morphism groups is additive. For the
3016	  semilinearity, recall that the global-scalar action of
3017	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom_value} acts on \(\varphi\) by post-composition with
3018	  the multiplication-by-\(f\) endomorphism \(m_f^{N}\); restricting that endomorphism
3019	  from \(U\) to \(V\) is multiplication by \(f|_V = R(g)(f)\) on \(N|_V\). Since further
3020	  restriction commutes with composition of morphisms, restricting \(f \cdot \varphi =
3021	  m_f^{N} \circ \varphi\) yields \(m_{R(g)(f)}^{N|_V} \circ (\varphi|_V) = R(g)(f) \cdot
3022	  (\varphi|_V)\), which is the asserted compatibility.
3023	\end{proof}

3025	\begin{definition}
3026	
3027	\leanok
3028	[Presheaf dual against the structure presheaf]
3029	  \label{def:presheaf_dual}
3030	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.dual}
3031	  \uses{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
3032	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as in \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}, the
3033	  \emph{presheaf dual} of \(M\) is the internal hom into the unit (structure)
3034	  presheaf,
3035	  \[
3036	    M^\vee \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om(M, R),
3037	  \]
3038	  i.e.\ \(M^\vee(U) = \mathtt{ModuleCat.of}\,(R(U))\,(M|_U \to R|_U)\), the
3039	  \(R(U)\)-module of \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\). This is the
3040	  presheaf-of-modules analogue of the linear dual
3041	  \(\mathtt{Module.Dual}\,R\,M = (M \to R)\) that serves as the \(\otimes\)-inverse of
3042	  an invertible module over a fixed ring; here the fixed-ring dual is taken
3043	  open-by-open and assembled by the slice presentation of
3044	  \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}.
3045	\end{definition}

3067	  % \mathcal{F}
3068	  % \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_X}
3069	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
3070	  % \longrightarrow
3071	  % \mathcal{G}.
3072	  % $$"
3073	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
3074	  (tag area 01CM).}
3075	  With \(R\) and \(M\) as above, there is a canonical \emph{evaluation} (contraction)
3076	  morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules
3077	  \[
3078	    \mathrm{ev}_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_R M^\vee \;\longrightarrow\; R,
3079	    \qquad s \otimes \phi \longmapsto \phi(s),
3080	  \]
3081	  the counit of the tensor--hom adjunction
3082	  \(\mathcal{H}om(M \otimes_R -, R) \cong \mathcal{H}om(-, M^\vee)\) specialised at
3083	  \(R\). On each open \(U\) it is the open-by-open contraction
3084	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R|_U} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R|_U\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto
3085	  \phi(s)\), and these are compatible with restriction, so they assemble to a morphism
3086	  of presheaves of modules. The tensor product is the presheaf tensor underlying
3087	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.

3089	
3090	\begin{proof}
3091	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}
3092	  On a fixed open \(U\), the value \(M^\vee(U) = (M|_U \to R|_U)\) is the module of
3093	  \(R|_U\)-linear functionals on \(M|_U\), and the evaluation
3094	  \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\) is the standard contraction
3095	  \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\) bilinear over \(R(U)\), hence a
3096	  well-defined \(R(U)\)-linear map out of the tensor product. For \(V \subseteq U\)
3097	  the square relating the \(U\)- and \(V\)-contractions commutes because evaluating
3098	  then restricting equals restricting both arguments then evaluating
3099	  (\(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\)); hence the open-by-open contractions are natural
3100	  in the restriction maps and define a morphism of presheaves of \(R\)-modules.
3101	  Concretely, the morphism is defined by specifying, on each open \(U\), the per-open
3102	  contraction \((M|_U) \otimes_{R(U)} (M|_U \to R|_U) \to R(U)\), \(s \otimes \phi \mapsto \phi(s)\).
3103	  The single remaining datum is the naturality square, which reduces to the
3104	  evaluate/restrict-commutation identity \(\phi(s)|_V = (\phi|_V)(s|_V)\) above: the
3105	  restriction \(\phi|_V\) of a functional inside the dual \(M^\vee\) is its restriction
3106	  to the open \(V\), and restriction is functorial, so evaluating the restricted
3107	  functional on the restricted section agrees with restricting the value of the
3108	  evaluation. This is the same restriction-functoriality argument already used to make
3109	  the internal-hom restriction maps functorial in
3110	  \cref{lem:presheaf_internal_hom_restriction}. This is the counit of the tensor--hom
3111	  adjunction of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom} specialised to the unit presheaf.
3112	\end{proof}

3114	\begin{lemma}
3115	
3116	\leanok
3117	[The internal hom is a sheaf; the sheaf-level dual]
3118	  \label{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}
3119	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual}
3120	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
3121	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom
3122	  % (\texttt{section-internal-hom}, tag area 01CM)
3123	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3502--L3514).
3124	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3125	  % "Consider the rule
3126	  % $$
3127	  % U \longmapsto \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X|_U}(\mathcal{F}|_U, \mathcal{G}|_U).
3128	  % $$
3129	  % It follows from the discussion in Sheaves, Section
3130	  % \ref{sheaves-section-glueing-sheaves} that this is a sheaf of
3131	  % abelian groups. ... Hence we in fact get a sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules."
3132	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom
3133	  (tag area 01CM).}
3134	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, \(R = X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\), and let
3135	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with \(N\) a sheaf of modules. Then the
3136	  presheaf internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) of \cref{def:presheaf_internal_hom}
3137	  satisfies the sheaf condition: for an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\), a family of
3138	  morphisms \(M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) agreeing on the overlaps
3139	  \(U_i \cap U_j\) glues to a unique morphism \(M|_U \to N|_U\), because \(N\) is a
3140	  sheaf and morphisms of sheaves of modules are determined and gluable section-wise.
3141	  Hence \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) descends to an object of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
3142	  Specialising to \(N = \mathcal{O}_X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\,R\) gives the
3143	  \emph{sheaf-level dual}
3144	  \[
3145	    \mathtt{dual}\,M \;:=\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X)
3146	    \;\in\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X,
3147	  \]
3148	  and the evaluation \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval} descends to a morphism of sheaves of
3149	  modules \(M \otimes_X \mathtt{dual}\,M \to \mathcal{O}_X\).
3150	\end{lemma}
3151	
3152	\begin{proof}
3153	  \uses{def:presheaf_dual, lem:internal_hom_eval}
3154	  Fix an open \(U\) and an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(U\). A morphism
3155	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) is a section of \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\) over \(U\); restricting it
3156	  to the \(U_i\) gives a compatible family. Conversely, given morphisms
3157	  \(\varphi_i : M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) with \(\varphi_i|_{U_i \cap U_j} =
3158	  \varphi_j|_{U_i \cap U_j}\), section-wise gluing in the sheaf \(N\) produces, for
3159	  each open \(W \subseteq U\) and section \(s \in M(W)\), a well-defined section of
3160	  \(N(W)\) by gluing the \(\varphi_i(s|_{W \cap U_i})\) (these agree on overlaps by
3161	  hypothesis, and \(N\) is a sheaf so the glued section is unique). The resulting
3162	  assignment is \(R(W)\)-linear and natural in \(W\), hence a morphism
3163	  \(M|_U \to N|_U\) restricting to the \(\varphi_i\); uniqueness is the separatedness
3164	  of \(N\). This is exactly the sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{H}om(M, N)\), so it is a
3165	  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Taking \(N = \mathcal{O}_X\) yields
3166	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,M\), and the evaluation morphism of \cref{lem:internal_hom_eval},
3167	  being a morphism of presheaves into the sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\), is a morphism of
3168	  the corresponding sheaves of modules.
3169	\end{proof}

3179	  Let \(e : R \simeq S\) be an isomorphism of commutative rings, and let \(M\) be an
3180	  \(S\)-module. Restriction of scalars along \(e\) commutes with the formation of the
3181	  linear dual: the canonical map
3182	  \[
3183	    \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e\bigl(M \to_S S\bigr)
3184	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3185	    \bigl(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M \to_R R\bigr),
3186	    \qquad \varphi \;\longmapsto\; e^{-1} \circ \varphi,
3187	  \]
3188	  is an \(R\)-linear equivalence, with inverse \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\). On both
3189	  sides the \(R\)-module structure is the \(S\)-module structure of \(M\) pulled back
3190	  along \(e\), so that \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\): the left-hand side is the
3191	  \(S\)-linear dual \((M \to_S S)\) regarded as an \(R\)-module by restriction, and the
3192	  right-hand side is the \(R\)-linear dual of the restricted module
3193	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e M\) into the restricted ground ring. This is the dual
3194	  analogue of \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}: it is to the internal hom
3195	  / dual exactly what that lemma is to the tensor product. It is the \emph{H2$'$
3196	  ingredient} of the restriction-compatibility isomorphism of the C-bridge
3197	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial} --- the ring-iso/dual commutation that, composed with
3198	  the open-immersion pushforward comparison H1, identifies the dual of a restriction
3199	  with the restriction of the dual.
3200	\end{lemma}

3202	\begin{proof}
3203	  The two assignments \(\varphi \mapsto e^{-1} \circ \varphi\) and
3204	  \(\psi \mapsto e \circ \psi\) are mutually inverse, since \(e \circ e^{-1} =
3205	  \mathrm{id}_S\) and \(e^{-1} \circ e = \mathrm{id}_R\) as ring homomorphisms, so the
3206	  two composites are the identity on functionals. Each assignment is \(R\)-linear:
3207	  additivity is immediate, and for a scalar \(r \in R\) the defining relation
3208	  \(r \cdot m = e(r) \cdot m\) of the pulled-back module structure makes each linearity
3209	  check a single rewrite --- \(e^{-1}\) (resp.\ \(e\)) intertwines the \(R\)-action on

3210	  the source with the \(R\)-action on the target precisely because \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\)
3211	  are inverse ring homomorphisms identifying \(R\) with \(S\). The forward map is
3212	  therefore an \(R\)-linear bijection, i.e.\ an \(R\)-linear equivalence; packaging the
3213	  two one-sided maps with the inverse identities gives
3214	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoDualEquiv}\).

3281	\begin{lemma}

3283	\leanok
3284	[Open-immersion slice-site sheaf equivalence (the shared slice bridge)]
3285	  \label{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
3286	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overSliceSheafEquiv}
3287	  % SOURCE: Mathlib code-location provenance (this is NOT an external literature
3288	  % citation, so it carries no verbatim mathematical SOURCE QUOTE). This lemma
3289	  % completes the documented TODO at Mathlib/Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean, lines
3290	  % 19--22, reproduced here for orientation:
3291	  %   "show that both functors of the equivalence overEquivalence U are continuous
3292	  %    and induce an equivalence between Sheaf ((Opens.grothendieckTopology X).over U) A
3293	  %    and Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology U) A for any category A."
3294	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open subset, with associated open
3295	  immersion \(\iota : U \hookrightarrow X\). The reindexing equivalence
3296	  \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U : \mathtt{Over}\,U \simeq \mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\)
3297	  --- between the slice of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) over \(U\) and the opens of the subspace
3298	  \(\widetilde{U}\) --- upgrades to an \emph{equivalence of sheaf categories}
3299	  \[
3300	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl((\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,X).\mathtt{over}\,U\bigr)\,A
3301	      \;\;\simeq\;\;
3302	    \mathtt{Sheaf}\bigl(\mathtt{Opens.grothendieckTopology}\,U\bigr)\,A
3303	  \]
3304	  for any target category \(A\), compatible with the module-pullback comparison
3305	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). This completes the Mathlib TODO recorded at
3306	  \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} (lines 19--22). It is the \emph{shared root}
3307	  underlying both the restriction-compatibility isomorphism for the dual
3308	  (\cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}) and the morphism-descent / gluing engine
3309	  (\cref{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}): both reduce to identifying a
3310	  slice-indexed family of sections over \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) with an
3311	  open-immersion-indexed family over \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\), and that
3312	  identification is exactly this equivalence.
3313	\end{lemma}
3314	
3315	\begin{proof}
3316	  The functor \(\mathtt{overEquivalence}\,U\) realises \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\) as a
3317	  \emph{dense subsite} of \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U}\): every open of the subspace
3318	  \(\widetilde{U}\) is covered by down-sets \(\{V : V \le U\}\), and these down-sets are
3319	  precisely the objects of the slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U\). One therefore applies the
3320	  dense-subsite transfer \(\mathtt{Functor.IsDenseSubsite.sheafEquiv}\): a continuous,
3321	  cocontinuous, dense inclusion of sites induces an equivalence of the associated sheaf
3322	  categories. The continuity and cover-lifting hypotheses are supplied by the slice-site
3323	  instances of \(\mathtt{Sites/Over}\) (the Grothendieck topology
3324	  \(J.\mathtt{over}\,U\) and its comparison with the topology pulled back along
3325	  \(\iota\)).
3326	
3327	  The over-category coherence that is laborious for a general site --- the pseudofunctor
3328	  identities \(\mathtt{Over.mapId}\), \(\mathtt{Over.mapComp}\) and the associativity
3329	  \(\mathtt{overMapPullback\_assoc}\) --- is here \emph{automatic}: because
3330	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) is a thin poset, every hom-set is a subsingleton, so each such
3331	  square commutes by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\) (and its heterogeneous form
3332	  \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.helim}\) where source and target objects differ only up to a
3333	  propositional equality of opens). The sole sectionwise content is the down-set
3334	  identification
3335	  \[
3336	    \iota_{!}\bigl(\iota^{-1}(V)\bigr) \;=\; V
3337	    \qquad (V \le U),
3338	  \]
3339	  i.e.\ the image under the open immersion of the preimage of an open contained in \(U\)
3340	  is that open again. Transporting sections along this equality and invoking the
3341	  dense-subsite equivalence yields the stated sheaf equivalence, compatible with
3342	  \(\mathtt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) by the same thinness argument.
3343	\end{proof}

3345	\begin{lemma}
3346	[Restriction commutes with the dual along an open immersion (the C-bridge)]
3347	  \label{lem:dual_restrict_iso}
3348	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.dual_restrict_iso}
3349	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
3350	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Modules on Ringed Spaces", \S Internal Hom,
3351	  % Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM)
3352	  % (read from references/stacks-modules.tex, L3710--L3721).
3353	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
3354	  % "Let $f : (X, \mathcal{O}_X) \to (Y, \mathcal{O}_Y)$ be a morphism
3355	  % of ringed spaces. Let $\mathcal{F}$, $\mathcal{G}$ be $\mathcal{O}_Y$-modules.
3356	  % If $\mathcal{F}$ is finitely presented and $f$ is flat,
3357	  % then the canonical map
3358	  % $$
3359	  % f^*\SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G})
3360	  % \longrightarrow
3361	  % \SheafHom_{\mathcal{O}_X}(f^*\mathcal{F}, f^*\mathcal{G})
3362	  % $$
3363	  % is an isomorphism."
3364	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], ``Modules on Ringed Spaces'', \S Internal Hom,
3365	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom} (tag area 01CM).}
3366	  Let \(f : Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes, with image the open
3367	  \(U \subseteq X\), and let \(M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Then there is a
3368	  canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-modules
3369	  \[
3370	    (\mathtt{dual}\,M)\big|_f \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(M|_f\bigr),
3371	  \]
3372	  natural in \(M\), between the restriction of the sheaf-level dual
3373	  (\cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf}) and the dual of the restriction. (Only opens
3374	  \(V \subseteq U\) intervene downstream, where the comparison is needed.)
3375	\end{lemma}
3376	
3377	\begin{proof}
3378	  \uses{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
3379	  This is the classical internal-hom base-change isomorphism
3380	  \(f^*\mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(M, \mathcal{O}_X) \cong
3381	  \mathcal{H}\!om_{\mathcal{O}_Y}(f^*M, f^*\mathcal{O}_X)\) of
3382	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-pullback-internal-hom}, specialised to the structure sheaf
3383	  \(\mathcal{G} = \mathcal{O}_X\). For an open immersion the morphism \(f\) is flat and
3384	  restriction \((-)|_f\) is the exact pullback \(f^*\), so the finite-presentation and
3385	  flatness hypotheses of the general ringed-space statement are automatic; this is the
3386	  \emph{favorable} (open-immersion) case of the base-change comparison, in which the map
3387	  is an isomorphism for \emph{every} \(M\), not only the finitely-presented ones.
3388	
3389	  We emphasise at the outset that this is a genuine \emph{natural isomorphism}, not a
3390	  definitional equality: the two sides are not equal on the nose --- they carry
3391	  \emph{different module structures} that a sectionwise ring isomorphism must reconcile
3392	  --- so the comparison must be constructed and its naturality verified, even though the
3393	  underlying additive groups of functionals coincide. The construction lives at the
3394	  \emph{presheaf-of-modules level}, where the residual genuinely sits, and is carried out
3395	  objectwise in the open \(V\) (with \(V \subseteq U\)) from three ingredients.
3396	
3397	  \emph{(a) The per-\(V\) slice equivalence.} Because \(f\) is an open immersion, its
3398	  direct-image-of-opens functor \(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\) is fully faithful with image
3399	  the down-set \(\{W : W \le U\}\); consequently, for each open \(V \subseteq U\) there is
3400	  an equivalence of slice categories
3401	  \[
3402	    \mathtt{Over}_Y\,V \;\simeq\; \mathtt{Over}_X\bigl(f.\mathtt{opensFunctor}\,V\bigr),
3403	  \]
3404	  the per-open shadow of the reindexing equivalence
3405	  \(\mathtt{TopologicalSpace.Opens.overEquivalence}\). This identifies the indexing slice
3406	  over which the internal hom on \(Y\) computes its value at \(V\) with the corresponding
3407	  slice over \(X\). This is a per-open, presheaf-level instrument, working one open \(V\)
3408	  at a time and respecting the varying-ring \(\mathcal{O}(V)\)-module structure that the
3409	  dual carries; the comparison is built entirely at this presheaf-of-modules level, where
3410	  the residual genuinely lives.
3411	
3412	  \emph{(b) Agreement of the codomain.} The codomain of the internal hom on both sides is
3413	  the structure sheaf. On opens \(V \subseteq U\) the structure sheaf of \(Y\) agrees with
3414	  that of \(X\) under the identification of (a) --- \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V) =
3415	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV)\) up to the canonical comparison --- so the target side of the hom
3416	  matches under the slice reindexing.
3417	
3418	  \emph{(c) The ring-iso transport of the module structure.} The only remaining
3419	  discrepancy is the module structure. The left-hand side
3420	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,M)|_f\) carries, at \(V\), the \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-module structure;
3421	  the right-hand side \(\mathtt{dual}(M|_f)\) carries the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-module
3422	  structure. The sectionwise comparison
3423	  \(\beta_V = (\mathtt{toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathtt{hom}_V \colon
3424	  \mathcal{O}_X(fV) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_Y(V)\) is a ring \emph{isomorphism}, so
3425	  restriction of scalars along \(\beta_V\) is an \emph{equivalence}
3426	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalars\_isEquivalence\_of\_ringEquiv}\)). Its ModuleCat-level shadow
3427	  on duals is exactly \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}: it carries the
3428	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(fV)\)-linear dual onto the \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear dual,
3429	  \(R(V)\)-linearly and invertibly.
3430	
3431	  Composing (a), (b) and (c) gives, for each \(V \subseteq U\), an
3432	  \(\mathcal{O}_Y(V)\)-linear isomorphism between the two values, and one checks

3460	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(L \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) with
3461	  \(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\,L\). Then the sheaf-level dual
3462	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) of \cref{lem:internal_hom_isSheaf} is again locally trivial of

3468	        def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso, lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}
3469	  Local triviality of \(L\) furnishes, around each point, an affine open \(U\) with a
3470	  trivialisation \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\); writing \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) for the
3471	  associated open immersion, it suffices to exhibit an isomorphism
3472	  \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). This is the following three-step chain:
3473	  \[
3474	    (\mathtt{dual}\,L)\big|_f
3475	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\bigl(L|_f\bigr)
3476	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U
3477	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \mathcal{O}_U.
3478	  \]
3479	  \begin{enumerate}
3480	    \item The first isomorphism is the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
3481	      \cref{lem:dual_restrict_iso}: restriction along the open immersion \(f\) commutes
3482	      with the formation of the dual. This is the C-bridge proper; its substantive content
3483	      is the sectionwise ring-iso transport of the module structure
3484	      (\cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_dualequiv}), as detailed there.
3485	    \item The second isomorphism is the dual applied to the trivialisation
3486	      \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\), via the contravariant functoriality of the sheaf-level
3487	      dual in isomorphisms (\(\mathtt{dualIsoOfIso}\),
3488	      \cref{def:scheme_modules_dual_iso_of_iso}): an isomorphism \(L|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\)
3489	      induces \(\mathtt{dual}(L|_U) \cong \mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U\).
3490	    \item The third isomorphism trivialises the dual of the unit,
3491	      \(\mathtt{dual}\,\mathcal{O}_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (\(\mathtt{dual\_unit\_iso}\)):
3492	      the dual of the structure sheaf is the structure sheaf itself, the comparison being
3493	      evaluation at \(1 \in \mathcal{O}_U\), under which
3494	      \(\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_U}(\mathcal{O}_U, \mathcal{O}_U) \cong \mathcal{O}_U\).
3495	  \end{enumerate}
3496	  Composing the three exhibits \((\mathtt{dual}\,L)|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). Hence
3497	  \(\mathtt{dual}\,L\) is locally trivial of rank one, as required by item~2 of Stacks
3498	  Lemma~\texttt{lemma-constructions-invertible} (tag 01CR).

3507	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi \colon M \to N\) a morphism in
3508	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\).
3509	  Suppose that for every point \(x \in X\) there is an open neighbourhood \(U_x\) of
3510	  \(x\) such that the restriction of \(\varphi\) to \(U_x\) --- the image
3511	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) under the
3512	  restriction functor along the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\colon U_x \to X\) ---
3513	  is an isomorphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_x\). Then \(\varphi\) is an
3514	  isomorphism.

3517	\begin{proof}
3518	  Being an isomorphism for a morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules can be
3519	  checked on the underlying morphism of sheaves of abelian groups, and there it can be
3520	  checked stalkwise. Fix a point \(x \in X\). Choose a preimage \(x' \in U_x\) of \(x\)
3521	  under the open immersion \((U_x).\mathtt{i}\). By hypothesis the restriction
3522	  \((\mathtt{restrictFunctor}\,(U_x).\mathtt{i}).\mathtt{map}\,\varphi\) is an
3523	  isomorphism, hence so is its image under any functor --- in particular under the
3524	  stalk-at-\(x'\) functor on the underlying ab-sheaves. The natural isomorphism
3525	  ``restriction commutes with stalks'' (\(\mathtt{restrictStalkNatIso}\)) identifies
3526	  this with the stalk of (the underlying ab-sheaf morphism of) \(\varphi\) at the image
3527	  point \(x\); transporting along it shows that stalk is an isomorphism. As this holds
3528	  at every \(x\), and a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups all of whose stalks are
3529	  isomorphisms is itself an isomorphism (stalkwise iso-detection,
3530	  \(\mathtt{isIso\_of\_stalkFunctor\_map\_iso}\) on \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\)), the
3531	  underlying ab-sheaf morphism of \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism. Finally the forgetful
3532	  functor \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules.toPresheaf}\) reflects isomorphisms, so \(\varphi\)
3533	  itself is an isomorphism.
3534	\end{proof}

3560	  \label{lem:sheafofmodules_hom_of_local_compat}

3563	  open cover of \(X\). Suppose given, for each \(i\), a morphism
3564	  \(f_i \colon M|_{U_i} \to N|_{U_i}\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,U_i\) such that
3565	  for all \(i, j\) the restrictions of \(f_i\) and \(f_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree.
3566	  Then there is a unique global morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
3567	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\).
3568	\end{lemma}

3570	\begin{proof}
3571	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_homMk, lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}
3572	  A morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is, concretely, a morphism of the
3573	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups that is in addition \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear
3574	  sectionwise. We build \(f\) in two steps.
3575	
3576	  \emph{(i) Glue the underlying ab-sheaf morphism.} Forget \(M\) and \(N\) to their
3577	  underlying sheaves of abelian groups. The morphisms-between-them presheaf
3578	  \(\mathcal{H} := \mathtt{presheafHom}\,(M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\,
3579	  (N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf})\), whose value at an open \(W\) is the abelian
3580	  group of morphisms \(M|_W \to N|_W\) of presheaves of abelian groups, is itself a
3581	  \emph{sheaf of types} --- this is the ``hom into a sheaf is a sheaf'' principle
3582	  \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsSheaf.hom}\), which consumes the sheaf condition of \(N\).
3583	  Regarding \(\mathcal{H}\) as a \(\mathtt{TopCat.Sheaf}\), its universal gluing
3584	  property \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) amalgamates any compatible family of local
3585	  sections of \(\mathcal{H}\) into a unique global section. This route is preferred over
3586	  the raw sieve-level amalgamation \(\mathtt{presheafHom\_isSheafFor}\), which carries
3587	  strictly more bookkeeping.
3588	
3589	  The load-bearing datum is, for each \(i\), the \emph{local section}
3590	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i \in \mathcal{H}.\mathrm{obj}\,(\mathrm{op}\,U_i)\)
3591	  manufactured from \(f_i\). Its component at a pair \((V, h : V \to U_i)\) is the given
3592	  component of \(f_i\), conjugated by the canonical \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\) identifications
3593	  that arise from the open-set equality \(\iota_i\bigl(\iota_i^{-1}(V)\bigr) = V\) valid
3594	  for \(V \subseteq U_i\) (the image under the open immersion \(\iota_i : U_i \to X\) of
3595	  the preimage of \(V\) is \(V\) again). The genuine coherence risk of the whole
3596	  construction is concentrated in the \emph{naturality field} of
3597	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\,i\): the requirement that these
3598	  \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugated components of \(f_i\) commute across the morphisms of
3599	  the slice category \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\). This is the one sub-piece that should be
3600	  built and verified first, as a standalone axiom-clean lemma.
3601	
3602	  This \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality is precisely the \emph{section-direction
3603	  slice} of the comparison packaged in \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}:
3604	  converting the local datum \(f_i\), which lives over the open-immersion pullback
3605	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,\widetilde{U_i}\), into a section of \(\mathcal{H}\) indexed over the
3606	  slice \(\mathtt{Over}\,U_i\) is the one-sided realisation of the open-immersion
3607	  \(\leftrightarrow\) slice transport. As there, the \(\mathtt{eqToHom}\)-conjugation is
3608	  controlled by the down-set identity \(\iota_i(\iota_i^{-1}(V)) = V\) for
3609	  \(V \subseteq U_i\), and the coherence is automatic on the thin poset
3610	  \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\) by \(\mathtt{Subsingleton.elim}\). This A-engine uses the
3611	  sheaf-site equivalence \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv}; the C-bridge
3612	  \cref{lem:dual_isLocallyTrivial}, by contrast, uses a per-open slice comparison at
3613	  the presheaf level over the \emph{varying} section ring \(\mathcal{O}_X(V)\), and so
3614	  does \emph{not} reduce to the same sheaf-site root --- the two are independent and
3615	  must be built separately.
3616	
3617	  The remaining four sub-steps are mechanical: (a) the
3618	  cocycle/\(\mathtt{IsCompatible}\) condition on the family
3619	  \((\mathtt{localSection}\,i)_i\), which is exactly the assumed agreement of \(f_i\)
3620	  and \(f_j\) on the overlaps \(U_i \cap U_j\); (b) gluing via
3621	  \(\mathtt{existsUnique\_gluing}\) and converting the amalgamated section over
3622	  \(\mathrm{op}\,\top\) into a genuine morphism \(g \colon M.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}
3623	  \to N.\mathtt{val}.\mathtt{presheaf}\) of the underlying presheaves --- the
3624	  \(\top\)-section-to-morphism passage being the global-sections equivalence
3625	  \(\mathtt{presheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) / \(\mathtt{sheafHomSectionsEquiv}\) of the
3626	  hom-sheaf \(\mathcal{H}\) (which, since \(\top\) is terminal in \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\),
3627	  lands the glued section as an honest morphism of presheaves), \emph{not} a hand-unfold
3628	  of the \(\top\)-section; (c) the
3629	  sectionwise linearity check; and (d) the recovery that the restriction of \(g\) to
3630	  each \(U_i\) is the ab-sheaf morphism underlying \(f_i\).
3631	
3632	  \emph{(ii) Promote to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear.} The linearity equation
3633	  \(g(r \cdot m) = r \cdot g(m)\) is a sectionwise identity between two sections of a
3634	  separated presheaf. It holds on each \(U_i\), because there \(g\) restricts to the
3635	  module morphism \(f_i\); since the two sides agree on the cover \(\{U_i\}\) and the
3636	  ambient presheaf is separated, they agree globally. Packaging \(g\) together with this
3637	  global linearity through the scheme-level wrapper \cref{def:scheme_modules_homMk}
3638	  (\(\mathtt{homMk}\)) yields a morphism \(f \colon M \to N\) in
3639	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\); its restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(f_i\) and its
3640	  uniqueness are both inherited from the ab-sheaf amalgamation of step (i).
3641	
3642	  This gluing engine is substantially larger than a routine glue: it is dominated by the
3643	  \(\mathtt{localSection}\) naturality field and the convert-and-recover bookkeeping, and
3644	  the naturality is the section-direction slice of
3645	  \cref{lem:open_immersion_slice_sheaf_equiv} --- so its true cost is bounded below by
3646	  building that sheaf-site equivalence, not by the surrounding assembly. It is therefore to be
3647	  scoped as a multi-piece build --- with \(\mathtt{localSection}\), together with its
3648	  naturality, built as a standalone axiom-clean lemma first --- rather than a one-shot.
3649	  The step computes no tensor stalk --- it is purely a statement about gluing morphisms
3650	  of sheaves --- and so it does not re-enter the abandoned tensor-stalk commutation.
